entry.title: Topology
entry.content:
Basic Concepts

Topology Space

A topology space is a set XXX with a collection of open sets T\mathcal{T}T that satisfy the following properties:

  1. ∅∈T\emptyset \in \mathcal{T}∅∈T and X∈TX \in \mathcal{T}X∈T.
  2. if {Ui}i∈I⊆T\{U_i\}_{i \in I} \subseteq \mathcal{T}{Ui​}i∈I​⊆T, then ⋃i∈IUi∈T\bigcup_{i \in I} U_i \in \mathcal{T}⋃i∈I​Ui​∈T.
  3. if U1,U2∈TU_1, U_2 \in \mathcal{T}U1​,U2​∈T, then U1∩U2∈TU_1 \cap U_2 \in \mathcal{T}U1​∩U2​∈T.

A topological space is denoted as (X,T)(X, \mathcal{T})(X,T).

Basis and subbasis for a Topology

A basis B\mathcal{B}B for a topology on XXX is a collection of sets such that:

  1. For each x∈Xx \in Xx∈X, there exists a basis element B∈BB \in \mathcal{B}B∈B such that x∈Bx \in Bx∈B.
  2. If B1,B2∈BB_1, B_2 \in \mathcal{B}B1​,B2​∈B and x∈B1∩B2x \in B_1 \cap B_2x∈B1​∩B2​, then there exists a basis element B3∈BB_3 \in \mathcal{B}B3​∈B such that x∈B3⊆B1∩B2x \in B_3 \subseteq B_1 \cap B_2x∈B3​⊆B1​∩B2​.

A topology generated by a basis B\mathcal{B}B is the collection of all unions of elements of B\mathcal{B}B. Formally, T={U⊆X:U=⋃i∈IBi,Bi∈B,I is an index set}\mathcal{T} = \{ U \subseteq X : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} B_i, B_i \in \mathcal{B}, I \text{ is an index set} \}T={U⊆X:U=⋃i∈I​Bi​,Bi​∈B,I is an index set}.

A subbasis for a topology on XXX is a collection of sets S\mathcal{S}S such that the collection of finite intersections of elements of S\mathcal{S}S forms a basis for a topology on XXX. Formally, a subbasis S\mathcal{S}S satisfies:

  1. For each x∈Xx \in Xx∈X, there exists a subbasis element S∈SS \in \mathcal{S}S∈S such that x∈Sx \in Sx∈S.
  2. The collection of finite intersections of elements of S\mathcal{S}S generates a basis for the topology on XXX.

A topology generated by a subbasis S\mathcal{S}S is denoted as T={U⊆X:U=⋃i∈I⋂j=1niSij,Sij∈S,ni is finite,I is an index set}\mathcal{T} = \{ U \subseteq X : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} \bigcap_{j=1}^{n_i} S_{ij}, S_{ij} \in \mathcal{S}, n_i \text{ is finite}, I \text{ is an index set} \}T={U⊆X:U=⋃i∈I​⋂j=1ni​​Sij​,Sij​∈S,ni​ is finite,I is an index set}.

Countability, Compactness and Separation Axioms

A topological space is first countable if every point has a countable local base, meaning for each point x∈Xx \in Xx∈X, there exists a countable collection of open sets {Un}n=1∞\{U_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}{Un​}n=1∞​ such that for any open set UUU containing xxx, there exists some nnn such that Un⊆UU_n \subseteq UUn​⊆U.

A topological space is second countable if it has a countable base, meaning there exists a countable collection of open sets B\mathcal{B}B such that every open set in the topology can be expressed as a union of sets from B\mathcal{B}B.

A topological space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover, meaning for any collection of open sets {Ui}i∈I\{U_i\}_{i \in I}{Ui​}i∈I​ such that X=⋃i∈IUiX = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_iX=⋃i∈I​Ui​, there exists a finite subset J⊆IJ \subseteq IJ⊆I such that X=⋃j∈JUjX = \bigcup_{j \in J} U_jX=⋃j∈J​Uj​.

A topological space is T0T_0T0​ (Kolmogorov) if for any two distinct points x,y∈Xx, y \in Xx,y∈X, there exists an open set containing one of the points but not the other. Intuitively, this means that a pair of point can be one-sidely distinguished by open sets for one of them.

A topological space is T1T_1T1​ (Frechet) if for any two distinct points x,y∈Xx, y \in Xx,y∈X, there exist open sets UxU_xUx​ and UyU_yUy​ such that x∈Uxx \in U_xx∈Ux​ and y∉Uxy \notin U_xy∈/Ux​, and y∈Uyy \in U_yy∈Uy​ and x∉Uyx \notin U_yx∈/Uy​. Intuitively, this means that points can be one-sidely separated by open sets for both of them.

A topological space is T2T_2T2​ (Hausdorff) if for any two distinct points x,y∈Xx, y \in Xx,y∈X, there exist disjoint open sets UxU_xUx​ and UyU_yUy​ such that x∈Uxx \in U_xx∈Ux​ and y∈Uyy \in U_yy∈Uy​. This means that points can be separated by disjoint open sets.

A topological space is regular if it is T1T_1T1​ and for every point xxx and closed set FFF not containing xxx, there exist disjoint open sets UUU and VVV such that x∈Ux \in Ux∈U and F⊆VF \subseteq VF⊆V. This means that points can be separated from closed sets by disjoint open sets.

A topological space is normal if for any two disjoint closed sets AAA and BBB, there exist disjoint open sets UUU and VVV such that A⊆UA \subseteq UA⊆U and B⊆VB \subseteq VB⊆V. This means that closed sets can be separated by disjoint open sets.

A topological space is T3T_3T3​ (regular Hausdorff) if it is both regular and Hausdorff. This means that points can be separated from closed sets by disjoint open sets, and distinct points can be separated by disjoint open sets. T3T_3T3​ spaces are also normal.

Important properties of these separation axioms include:

  • T0⊆T1⊆T2⊆T3T_0 \subseteq T_1 \subseteq T_2 \subseteq T_3T0​⊆T1​⊆T2​⊆T3​.
  • A single point set is closed in a T1T_1T1​ space.
  • A T2T_2T2​ space is normal if it is also regular since a point is closed in a T2T_2T2​ space.

Functions and Homeomorphisms

A function f:X→Yf: X \to Yf:X→Y between two topological spaces (X,TX)(X, \mathcal{T}_X)(X,TX​) and (Y,TY)(Y, \mathcal{T}_Y)(Y,TY​) is open if for every open set U∈TXU \in \mathcal{T}_XU∈TX​, the image f(U)f(U)f(U) is open in TY\mathcal{T}_YTY​. In other words,

f(U)∈TY for all U∈TX.f(U) \in \mathcal{T}_Y \text{ for all } U \in \mathcal{T}_X.f(U)∈TY​ for all U∈TX​.

A function f:X→Yf: X \to Yf:X→Y is closed if for every closed set C∈TXC \in \mathcal{T}_XC∈TX​, the image f(C)f(C)f(C) is closed in TY\mathcal{T}_YTY​. In other words,

f(C)∈TY for all C∈TX.f(C) \in \mathcal{T}_Y \text{ for all } C \in \mathcal{T}_X.f(C)∈TY​ for all C∈TX​.

A function f:(X,TX)→(Y,TY)f: (X, \mathcal{T}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{T}_Y)f:(X,TX​)→(Y,TY​) between topological spaces is continuous if for every open set V∈TYV \in \mathcal{T}_YV∈TY​, the preimage f−1(V)f^{-1}(V)f−1(V) is open in TX\mathcal{T}_XTX​. In other words,

f−1(V)∈TX for all V∈TY.f^{-1}(V) \in \mathcal{T}_X \text{ for all } V \in \mathcal{T}_Y.f−1(V)∈TX​ for all V∈TY​.

A function f:(X,TX)→(Y,TY)f: (X, \mathcal{T}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{T}_Y)f:(X,TX​)→(Y,TY​) is a homeomorphism if it is a continuous bijection and its inverse f−1:(Y,TY)→(X,TX)f^{-1}: (Y, \mathcal{T}_Y) \to (X, \mathcal{T}_X)f−1:(Y,TY​)→(X,TX​) is also continuous. In other words, both fff and f−1f^{-1}f−1 are continuous functions.

A homeomorphism establishes a topological equivalence between the two spaces, meaning they have the same topological properties.

Connectedness and Path Connectedness

A topological space is connected if it cannot be expressed as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets. In other words, there are no two open sets UUU and VVV such that X=U∪VX = U \cup VX=U∪V, U∩V=∅U \cap V = \emptysetU∩V=∅, and both UUU and VVV are non-empty.

A topological space is path connected if for any two points x,y∈Xx, y \in Xx,y∈X, there exists a continuous function (path) f:[0,1]→Xf: [0, 1] \to Xf:[0,1]→X such that f(0)=xf(0) = xf(0)=x and f(1)=yf(1) = yf(1)=y. This means that there is a continuous path connecting any two points in the space.

A topological space is locally path connected if every point has a neighborhood base consisting of path connected sets. This means that for each point x∈Xx \in Xx∈X, there exists a collection of open sets {Un}n=1∞\{U_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}{Un​}n=1∞​ such that
for each nnn, UnU_nUn​ is path connected and contains xxx, and for any open set UUU containing xxx, there exists some nnn such that Un⊆UU_n \subseteq UUn​⊆U.

A topological space is locally connected if every point has a neighborhood base consisting of connected sets. This means that for each point x∈Xx \in Xx∈X, there exists a collection of open sets {Un}n=1∞\{U_n\}_{n=1}^{\infty}{Un​}n=1∞​ such that for each nnn, UnU_nUn​ is connected and contains xxx, and for any open set UUU containing xxx, there exists some nnn such that Un⊆UU_n \subseteq UUn​⊆U.

Common Topologies

Fineness and coarseness of topologies are important concepts in topology. A topology T1\mathcal{T}_1T1​ is finer than another topology T2\mathcal{T}_2T2​ on the same set XXX if every open set in T2\mathcal{T}_2T2​ is also an open set in T1\mathcal{T}_1T1​. In other words, T1⊇T2\mathcal{T}_1 \supseteq \mathcal{T}_2T1​⊇T2​. Conversely, T2\mathcal{T}_2T2​ is coarser than T1\mathcal{T}_1T1​ if T1\mathcal{T}_1T1​ is finer than T2\mathcal{T}_2T2​.

The discrete topology on a set XXX is the finest topology, where every subset of XXX is open. Formally, Tdiscrete=P(X)\mathcal{T}_{\text{discrete}} = \mathcal{P}(X)Tdiscrete​=P(X), the power set of XXX.

The indiscrete topology (or trivial topology) on a set XXX is the coarsest topology, where only the empty set and the entire set XXX are open. Formally, Tindiscrete={∅,X}\mathcal{T}_{\text{indiscrete}} = \{\emptyset, X\}Tindiscrete​={∅,X}.

The quotient topology on a set XXX with an equivalence relation ∼\sim∼ is defined as follows:

  1. The quotient set is X/∼={[x]:x∈X}X / \sim = \{ [x] : x \in X \}X/∼={[x]:x∈X}, where [x][x][x] is the equivalence class of xxx.
  2. A subset U⊆X/∼U \subseteq X / \simU⊆X/∼ is open in the quotient topology if and only if its preimage under the natural projection map π:X→X/∼\pi: X \to X / \simπ:X→X/∼ is open in XXX. That is, UUU is open if π−1(U)\pi^{-1}(U)π−1(U) is open in XXX.

In other words, the quotient topology is the coarsest topology on X/∼X / \simX/∼ such that the natural projection map π\piπ is continuous.

The product topology on a product of topological spaces {Xi}i∈I\{X_i\}_{i \in I}{Xi​}i∈I​ is defined as follows:

  1. The product space is X=∏i∈IXiX = \prod_{i \in I} X_iX=∏i∈I​Xi​.
  2. A subset U⊆XU \subseteq XU⊆X is open in the product topology if it can be expressed as a union of sets of the form ∏i∈IUi\prod_{i \in I} U_i∏i∈I​Ui​, where UiU_iUi​ is open in XiX_iXi​ for each i∈Ii \in Ii∈I, and Ui=XiU_i = X_iUi​=Xi​ for all but finitely many iii.
    In other words, the product topology is generated by the basis consisting of all products of open sets, where only finitely many factors are not the entire space.

The box topology on a product of topological spaces {Xi}i∈I\{X_i\}_{i \in I}{Xi​}i∈I​ is defined similarly to the product topology, but without the restriction that only finitely many factors can be different from the entire space:

  1. The box space is X=∏i∈IXiX = \prod_{i \in I} X_iX=∏i∈I​Xi​.
  2. A subset U⊆XU \subseteq XU⊆X is open in the box topology if it can be expressed as a union of sets of the form ∏i∈IUi\prod_{i \in I} U_i∏i∈I​Ui​, where UiU_iUi​ is open in XiX_iXi​ for each i∈Ii \in Ii∈I.
    In other words, the box topology is generated by the basis consisting of all products of open sets, where each factor can be any open set in the corresponding space.

The subspace topology on a subset Y⊆XY \subseteq XY⊆X of a topological space (X,T)(X, \mathcal{T})(X,T) is defined as follows:

  1. The subspace is YYY.
  2. A subset U⊆YU \subseteq YU⊆Y is open in the subspace topology if it can be expressed as U=Y∩VU = Y \cap VU=Y∩V for some open set V∈XV \in XV∈X. In other words, UUU is open in the subspace topology if it is the intersection of YYY with an open set in XXX.

entry.title: Differential Geometry
entry.content:
References

  • John Lee, Introduction to Smooth Manifolds

  • Loring Tu, An Introduction to Manifolds

  • Victor Guillemin and Alan Pollack, Differential Topology

Manifolds and its Structures

Topological Manifolds

Let MMM be a Hausdorff and second countable topological space that is locally homeomorphic to Euclidean space Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn. We say that MMM is a manifold of dimension nnn if ∀p∈M,∃U⊆M,∃ϕ:U→Rn\forall p \in M, \exists U \subseteq M,\exists \phi: U \to \mathbb{R}^n∀p∈M,∃U⊆M,∃ϕ:U→Rn such that ϕ\phiϕ is a homeomorphism onto its image and ϕ(U)\phi(U)ϕ(U) is an open subset of Rn\mathbb{R}^nRn. Sometime we denote it as MnM^nMn.

We denote (U,ϕ)(U,\phi)(U,ϕ) as a coordinate chart, ϕ\phiϕ as a coordinate map, and ϕ−1\phi^{-1}ϕ−1 as a parameterization of UUU.

Atlas and CkC^{k}Ck-structure

An atlas on a topological manifold MMM is a collection of coordinate charts A={(Uα,ϕα)}α∈I\mathcal{A} = \{(U_\alpha, \phi_\alpha)\}_{\alpha \in I}A={(Uα​,ϕα​)}α∈I​ where ⋃α∈IUα=M\bigcup_{\alpha \in I} U_\alpha = M⋃α∈I​Uα​=M.

Let (U,ϕ)(U,\phi)(U,ϕ) and (V,ψ)(V, \psi)(V,ψ) be two coordinate charts in the atlas A\mathcal{A}A. The transition map from (U,ϕ)(U, \phi)(U,ϕ) to (V,ψ)(V, \psi)(V,ψ) is defined as ψ∘ϕ−1:ϕ(U∩V)→ψ(U∩V)\psi \circ \phi^{-1}: \phi(U \cap V) \to \psi(U \cap V)ψ∘ϕ−1:ϕ(U∩V)→ψ(U∩V).

We call the transition map CkC^{k}Ck compatible if it is a CkC^{k}Ck function. And we say that the atlas A\mathcal{A}A is a CkC^{k}Ck-atlas if ∀(U,ϕ),(V,ψ)∈A,U∩V≠∅\forall (U, \phi), (V, \psi) \in \mathcal{A}, U\cap V \neq \emptyset∀(U,ϕ),(V,ψ)∈A,U∩V=∅, the transition map ψ∘ϕ−1∈Ck(ϕ(U∩V)∩ψ(U∩V))\psi \circ \phi^{-1}\in C^{k}(\phi(U \cap V)\cap \psi(U \cap V))ψ∘ϕ−1∈Ck(ϕ(U∩V)∩ψ(U∩V)).

We say (U,ϕ)(U, \phi)(U,ϕ) are CkC^{k}Ck-compatible with A\mathcal{A}A if it is CkC^{k}Ck compatible with every chart in A\mathcal{A}A.

Let A\mathcal{A}A be a CkC^{k}Ck-atlas on a topological manifold MMM. Maximal extension of A\mathcal{A}A: A‾={(U,ϕ):(U,ϕ) is Ck-compatible with A}\overline{\mathcal{A}} = \{(U, \phi) : (U, \phi) \text{ is } C^{k} \text{-compatible with } \mathcal{A}\}A={(U,ϕ):(U,ϕ) is Ck-compatible with A}. It is unique by definition and is a CkC^{k}Ck-atlas on MMM.

And it is not hard to prove that if A‾=B‾\overline{\mathcal{A}} = \overline{\mathcal{B}}A=B for another CkC^{k}Ck-atlas B\mathcal{B}B, then A∪B\mathcal{A} \cup \mathcal{B}A∪B is also a CkC^{k}Ck-atlas on MMM.

Actually, Let F\mathcal{F}F be the collection of all CkC^{k}Ck-compatible coordinate charts on MMM. Then (F,⊆)(\mathcal{F}, \subseteq)(F,⊆) is a directed set. And A‾\overline{\mathcal{A}}A is the maximal element of F\mathcal{F}F.

We call A‾\overline{\mathcal{A}}A a maximal CkC^{k}Ck-atlas or a CkC^{k}Ck-structure on MMM. A manifold with a CkC^{k}Ck-structure is called a CkC^{k}Ck-manifold.

Smooth Manifolds

A smooth manifold is a C∞C^{\infty}C∞-manifold.

Coordinate Functions

Let (U,ϕ)(U, \phi)(U,ϕ) be a coordinate chart on a nnn-manifold MMM. The coordinate functions are the components of the coordinate map ϕ:U→Rn\phi: U \to \mathbb{R}^nϕ:U→Rn, denoted as ϕ=(ϕ1,ϕ2,…,ϕn)\phi = (\phi^1, \phi^2, \ldots, \phi^n)ϕ=(ϕ1,ϕ2,…,ϕn). We call the coordinate functions ϕi:U→R\phi^i: U \to \mathbb{R}ϕi:U→R the iii-th coordinate function.

For p∈Up\in Up∈U, we write ϕ(p)=(x1,x2,…,xn)\phi(p) = (x^1, x^2, \ldots, x^n)ϕ(p)=(x1,x2,…,xn), where xi=ϕi(p)x^i = \phi^i(p)xi=ϕi(p).

And we call (x1,x2,…,xn)(x^1, x^2, \ldots, x^n)(x1,x2,…,xn) the local coordinates of ppp in the chart (U,ϕ)(U, \phi)(U,ϕ).

Tangent Vectors and Tangent Spaces

Germs of functions

A germ of a function at a point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M is an equivalence class of functions defined on a neighborhood of ppp. Two functions fff and ggg are equivalent if they agree on some neighborhood of ppp.

Formally, let (f,U)(f,U)(f,U) and (g,V)(g,V)(g,V) be two functions defined on neighborhoods UUU and VVV of ppp. If there exists a neighborhood W⊆U∩VW \subseteq U \cap VW⊆U∩V such that f∣W=g∣Wf|_W = g|_Wf∣W​=g∣W​, then we say that the germ of fff at ppp is equal to the germ of ggg at ppp, denoted as (f,U)∼(g,V)(f,U) \sim (g,V)(f,U)∼(g,V).

A germ of a function at ppp is denoted as [f]p[f]_p[f]p​, [f][f][f] or fff if the context is clear.

We denote Cp∞(M)C^{\infty}_p(M)Cp∞​(M) or Cp∞C^{\infty}_pCp∞​ as the set of germs of smooth functions at p∈Mp \in Mp∈M. Formally, Cp∞(M)={[f]p:f∈C∞(U),U is a neighborhood of p}C^{\infty}_p(M) = \{ [f]_p : f \in C^{\infty}(U), U \text{ is a neighborhood of } p \}Cp∞​(M)={[f]p​:f∈C∞(U),U is a neighborhood of p}.

If we write f∈Cp∞(M)f \in C^{\infty}_p(M)f∈Cp∞​(M), it means that [f]p∈Cp∞(M)[f]_p\in C^{\infty}_p(M)[f]p​∈Cp∞​(M).

Definition

There is three common definitions of tangent vectors on a manifold MMM:

  1. Tangent Vector as Derivation: A tangent vector at a point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M is a linear map v:Cp∞(M)→Rv: C^{\infty}_p(M) \to \mathbb{R}v:Cp∞​(M)→R that satisfies the Leibniz rule: v(fg)=v(f)g(p)+f(p)v(g)v(fg) = v(f)g(p) + f(p)v(g)v(fg)=v(f)g(p)+f(p)v(g) for all f,g∈Cp∞(M)f, g \in C^{\infty}_p(M)f,g∈Cp∞​(M).
  2. Tangent Vector as Equivalence Class of Curves: A tangent vector at a point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M is an equivalence class of curves γ:(−ϵ,ϵ)→M\gamma: (-\epsilon, \epsilon) \to Mγ:(−ϵ,ϵ)→M such that γ(0)=p\gamma(0) = pγ(0)=p.
  3. Tangent Vector as Derivative of Coordinate Functions: A tangent vector at a point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M is a vector in the tangent space TpMT_pMTp​M defined as the set of all derivations at ppp or equivalence classes of curves through ppp.

The tangent space at a point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M, denoted as TpMT_pMTp​M, is the set of all tangent vectors at ppp. It is a vector space over R\mathbb{R}R of dimension nnn, same as the dimension of the manifold MMM.

We shall mainly focus on the first definition of tangent vectors as derivations, which is the most general and abstract definition.

The tangent space is equipped with a natural R\mathbb{R}R-vector space structure.

Coordinate Expression

Let (U,x)=(U,x1,…,xn)(U,x)=(U,x^1,\ldots,x^n)(U,x)=(U,x1,…,xn) be a coordinate chart containing ppp.

Consider ∂∂xi∣p:Cp∞(M)→R\left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right|_p: C^{\infty}_p(M)\to \mathbb{R}∂xi∂​​p​:Cp∞​(M)→R act on f:U→Rf:U\to \mathbb Rf:U→R defined as

∂∂xi∣p([f])=∂(f∘x−1)∂xi∣x(p)\left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right|_p([f])=\left.\frac{\partial(f\circ x^{-1})}{\partial x^i}\right |_{x(p)}∂xi∂​​p​([f])=∂xi∂(f∘x−1)​​x(p)​

We know that {∂∂x1∣p,…,∂∂xn∣p}\left \{\left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^{1}}\right|_p,\ldots,\left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^n}\right|_p\right \}{∂x1∂​​p​,…,∂xn∂​​p​} forms a basis of TpMT_p MTp​M, $\dim T_pM = \dim M=n $.

By definition, we can prove the Kronecker delta property:

∂∂xi∣p(xj)=∂xj∘x−1∂xi∣x(p)=δij\left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right|_p(x^j) = \frac{\partial x^j\circ x^{-1}}{\partial x^i} \bigg|_{x(p)} = \delta^j_i ∂xi∂​​p​(xj)=∂xi∂xj∘x−1​​x(p)​=δij​

where δji\delta^i_jδji​ is the Kronecker delta, which is 111 if i=ji=ji=j and 000 otherwise.

So every Xp∈TpMX_p \in T_pMXp​∈Tp​M can be expressed as a linear combination of the basis vectors: Xp=∑i=1nXpi∂∂xi∣pX_p = \sum_{i=1}^n X_p^i \left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right|_pXp​=∑i=1n​Xpi​∂xi∂​​p​, where Xpi=Xp(xi)∈RX_p^i =X_p(x^i)\in \mathbb{R}Xpi​=Xp​(xi)∈R.

The coordinate expression of a tangent vector XpX_pXp​ at ppp in the chart (U,x)(U,x)(U,x) is given by the tuple (a1,a2,…,an)(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)(a1​,a2​,…,an​), where aia_iai​ are the coefficients in the linear combination.

Fiber Bundles

Definition of Fiber Bundle

A fiber bundle is a structure (E,M,π,F)(E, M, \pi, F)(E,M,π,F) consisting of:

  • Total space: EEE (a manifold)
  • Base space: MMM (a manifold)
  • Bundle projection: π:E→M\pi: E \to Mπ:E→M (a smooth surjective map)
  • Typical fiber: FFF (a manifold)

such that the local triviality condition holds: for each point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M, there exists an open neighborhood UUU of ppp and a diffeomorphism ϕ:π−1(U)→U×F\phi: \pi^{-1}(U) \to U \times Fϕ:π−1(U)→U×F satisfying:

pr1∘ϕ=π∣π−1(U)\text{pr}_1 \circ \phi = \pi|_{\pi^{-1}(U)} pr1​∘ϕ=π∣π−1(U)​

where pr1:U×F→U\text{pr}_1: U \times F \to Upr1​:U×F→U is the projection onto the first factor.

The pair (U,ϕ)(U, \phi)(U,ϕ) is called a local trivialization or bundle chart.

For each p∈Mp \in Mp∈M, the fiber over ppp is defined as Fp=π−1(p)F_p = \pi^{-1}(p)Fp​=π−1(p). The local triviality ensures that each fiber FpF_pFp​ is diffeomorphic to the typical fiber FFF.

Intuitively, a fiber bundle describes a space that ‘locally looks like a product’ but may have global twisting, like the Möbius strip over a circle.

Vector Bundles

A vector bundle is a fiber bundle (E,M,π,Rk)(E, M, \pi, \mathbb{R}^k)(E,M,π,Rk) where:

  1. Each fiber Fp=π−1(p)F_p = \pi^{-1}(p)Fp​=π−1(p) has the structure of a kkk-dimensional real vector space.
  2. The local trivializations ϕ:π−1(U)→U×Rk\phi: \pi^{-1}(U) \to U \times \mathbb{R}^kϕ:π−1(U)→U×Rk are linear on each fiber, meaning that for each p∈Up \in Up∈U, the restriction ϕ∣Fp:Fp→{p}×Rk≅Rk\phi|_{F_p}: F_p \to \{p\} \times \mathbb{R}^k \cong \mathbb{R}^kϕ∣Fp​​:Fp​→{p}×Rk≅Rk is a vector space isomorphism.

The integer kkk is called the rank of the vector bundle.

Tangent Bundle

The tangent bundle of a manifold MMM, denoted as TMTMTM, is the vector bundle whose total space is the disjoint union of all tangent spaces:

TM=⋃p∈MTpM={(p,Xp):p∈M,Xp∈TpM}TM = \bigcup_{p \in M} T_pM = \{(p, X_p) : p \in M, X_p \in T_pM\}TM=p∈M⋃​Tp​M={(p,Xp​):p∈M,Xp​∈Tp​M}

The bundle projection π:TM→M\pi: TM \to Mπ:TM→M is defined by π(p,Xp)=p\pi(p, X_p) = pπ(p,Xp​)=p.

Local Trivialization: Let (U,x=(x1,…,xn))(U, x = (x^1, \ldots, x^n))(U,x=(x1,…,xn)) be a coordinate chart on MMM. The tangent bundle can be locally trivialized over UUU by the map:

Φ:π−1(U)→U×Rn\Phi: \pi^{-1}(U) \to U \times \mathbb{R}^n Φ:π−1(U)→U×Rn

Φ(p,Xp)=(p,(Xp1,…,Xpn))\Phi(p, X_p) = \left(p, (X_p^1, \ldots, X_p^n)\right) Φ(p,Xp​)=(p,(Xp1​,…,Xpn​))

where Xp=∑i=1nXpi∂∂xi∣pX_p = \sum_{i=1}^n X_p^i \left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}\right|_pXp​=∑i=1n​Xpi​∂xi∂​​p​ is the coordinate representation of the tangent vector XpX_pXp​.

Manifold Structure: The tangent bundle TMTMTM is a smooth manifold of dimension 2n2n2n, where nnn is the dimension of MMM. The coordinate charts on TMTMTM are given by (U×Rn,Φ−1)(U \times \mathbb{R}^n, \Phi^{-1})(U×Rn,Φ−1) where (U,x)(U, x)(U,x) ranges over all coordinate charts on MMM.

Transition Maps: If (U,x)(U, x)(U,x) and (V,y)(V, y)(V,y) are two overlapping coordinate charts on MMM, the transition map between the corresponding bundle charts is:

ΦV∘ΦU−1:(U∩V)×Rn→(U∩V)×Rn\Phi_V \circ \Phi_U^{-1}: (U \cap V) \times \mathbb{R}^n \to (U \cap V) \times \mathbb{R}^n ΦV​∘ΦU−1​:(U∩V)×Rn→(U∩V)×Rn

(p,(v1,…,vn))↦(p,(∑i=1nvi∂yj∂xi∣p)j=1n)(p, (v^1, \ldots, v^n)) \mapsto \left(p, \left(\sum_{i=1}^n v^i \frac{\partial y^j}{\partial x^i}\bigg|_p\right)_{j=1}^n\right) (p,(v1,…,vn))↦​p,(i=1∑n​vi∂xi∂yj​​p​)j=1n​​

This map is smooth, confirming that TMTMTM has a smooth manifold structure.

Sections of Fiber Bundles

Definition of Sections

Let π:E→M\pi: E \to Mπ:E→M be a fiber bundle projection. A section of EEE is a map s:M→Es: M \to Es:M→E such that π∘s=idM\pi \circ s = \text{id}_Mπ∘s=idM​. In other words, for each point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M, we have s(p)∈Eps(p) \in E_ps(p)∈Ep​ (the fiber over ppp).

A section sss is called smooth if s:M→Es: M \to Es:M→E is a smooth map between manifolds.

Local Expression of Smoothness

Let (U,ϕ)(U, \phi)(U,ϕ) be a local trivialization of EEE over an open set U⊆MU \subseteq MU⊆M, where ϕ:π−1(U)→U×F\phi: \pi^{-1}(U) \to U \times Fϕ:π−1(U)→U×F for some typical fiber FFF. Any section sss over UUU can be written as:

ϕ∘s∣U:U→U×F\phi \circ s|_U: U \to U \times F ϕ∘s∣U​:U→U×F

which has the form p↦(p,f(p))p \mapsto (p, f(p))p↦(p,f(p)) for some function f:U→Ff: U \to Ff:U→F.

Sometimes we just say fff is a section of EEE over UUU.

Space of Sections

We denote by Γ(E)\Gamma(E)Γ(E) or Γ(M,E)\Gamma(M, E)Γ(M,E) the space of all smooth sections of the fiber bundle E→ME \to ME→M.

For General Fiber Bundles: The space Γ(E)\Gamma(E)Γ(E) has the structure of a set with pointwise operations (when they make sense on the typical fiber).

For Vector Bundles: When E→ME \to ME→M is a vector bundle with typical fiber Rk\mathbb{R}^kRk, the space Γ(E)\Gamma(E)Γ(E) has additional algebraic structures:

  1. Vector space structure: For sections s1,s2∈Γ(E)s_1, s_2 \in \Gamma(E)s1​,s2​∈Γ(E) and scalars a,b∈Ra, b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R:

    (as1+bs2)(p)=as1(p)+bs2(p)∈Ep(as_1 + bs_2)(p) = as_1(p) + bs_2(p) \in E_p (as1​+bs2​)(p)=as1​(p)+bs2​(p)∈Ep​

  2. C∞(M)C^\infty(M)C∞(M)-module structure: For a smooth function f∈C∞(M)f \in C^\infty(M)f∈C∞(M) and a section s∈Γ(E)s \in \Gamma(E)s∈Γ(E):

    (fs)(p)=f(p)⋅s(p)∈Ep(fs)(p) = f(p) \cdot s(p) \in E_p (fs)(p)=f(p)⋅s(p)∈Ep​

These structures exist because each fiber EpE_pEp​ is a vector space, allowing us to perform linear operations.

Differential

Let F:Mm→NnF: M^m \to N^nF:Mm→Nn be a smooth map between manifolds MMM and NNN. The differential of FFF at a point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M, denoted as dFp:TpM→TF(p)NdF_p: T_pM \to T_{F(p)}NdFp​:Tp​M→TF(p)​N, is a linear map between tangent spaces induced by the pushforward of FFF.

It is defined as follows: for any tangent vector Xp∈TpMX_p \in T_pMXp​∈Tp​M and any smooth function f∈CF(p)∞(N)f \in C^{\infty}_{F(p)}(N)f∈CF(p)∞​(N),

dFp(Xp)f=Xp(f∘F)dF_p(X_p)f = X_p(f \circ F) dFp​(Xp​)f=Xp​(f∘F)

Local Coordinate Expression: Let (U,x)(U, x)(U,x) and (V,y)(V, y)(V,y) be coordinate charts around ppp and F(p)F(p)F(p) respectively, and let F~=y∘F∘x−1\tilde F = y \circ F \circ x^{-1}F~=y∘F∘x−1 be the local representation of FFF. Then:

dFp(Xp)=∑j=1nXp(yj∘F)∂∂yj∣F(p)=∑j=1n∑i=1mXpi∂F~j∂xi∣x(p)∂∂yj∣F(p)=∑j=1n(∑i=1mXpi∂F~j∂xi∣x(p))∂∂yj∣F(p)\begin{align*}dF_p(X_p) &= \sum_{j=1}^n X_p(y^j\circ F) \frac{\partial}{\partial y^j} \bigg|_{F(p)}\\&= \sum_{j=1}^n \sum_{i=1}^m X_p^i \frac{\partial \tilde F^j}{\partial x^i} \bigg|_{x(p)} \frac{\partial}{\partial y^j} \bigg|_{F(p)}\\&= \sum_{j=1}^n \left(\sum_{i=1}^m X_p^i \frac{\partial \tilde F^j}{\partial x^i} \bigg|_{x(p)}\right) \frac{\partial}{\partial y^j} \bigg|_{F(p)}\end{align*}dFp​(Xp​)​=j=1∑n​Xp​(yj∘F)∂yj∂​​F(p)​=j=1∑n​i=1∑m​Xpi​∂xi∂F~j​​x(p)​∂yj∂​​F(p)​=j=1∑n​(i=1∑m​Xpi​∂xi∂F~j​​x(p)​)∂yj∂​​F(p)​​

In matrix form, if JF(p)=(∂F~j∂xi∣x(p))J_F(p) = \left(\frac{\partial \tilde F^j}{\partial x^i} \bigg|_{x(p)}\right)JF​(p)=(∂xi∂F~j​​x(p)​) is the Jacobian matrix of FFF at ppp, then:

dFp(Xp)=JF(p)XpdF_p(X_p) = J_F(p) X_p dFp​(Xp​)=JF​(p)Xp​

where XpX_pXp​ is considered as a column vector in local coordinates.

Vector Fields

A vector field on a manifold MMM is a smooth section of the tangent bundle TMTMTM. That is, a vector field XXX is a smooth map X:M→TMX: M \to TMX:M→TM such that π∘X=idM\pi \circ X = \text{id}_Mπ∘X=idM​, where π:TM→M\pi: TM \to Mπ:TM→M is the bundle projection.

Equivalently, a vector field assigns to each point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M a tangent vector Xp∈TpMX_p \in T_pMXp​∈Tp​M in a smooth manner.

Local Coordinate Expression:

The partial derivative operator ∂∂xi\frac{\partial}{\partial x^i}∂xi∂​ can be viewed as a vector field on MMM in the coordinate chart (U,x)(U, x)(U,x), where it acts on smooth functions f∈C∞(U)f \in C^\infty(U)f∈C∞(U) by:

∂f∂xi=d(xi∘f)=d(f∘x−1)∣x(p)\frac{\partial f}{\partial x^i} = d(x^i \circ f) = d(f \circ x^{-1}) \bigg|_{x(p)} ∂xi∂f​=d(xi∘f)=d(f∘x−1)​x(p)​

where xix^ixi is the iii-th coordinate function in the chart (U,x)(U, x)(U,x).

In a coordinate chart (U,x=(x1,…,xn))(U, x = (x^1, \ldots, x^n))(U,x=(x1,…,xn)), a vector field XXX can be expressed as:

X=∑i=1nXi∂∂xiX = \sum_{i=1}^n X^i \frac{\partial}{\partial x^i} X=i=1∑n​Xi∂xi∂​

where Xi:U→RX^i: U \to \mathbb{R}Xi:U→R are smooth functions called the components of the vector field XXX with respect to the coordinate chart (U,x)(U, x)(U,x).

The smoothness of the vector field XXX is equivalent to the smoothness of all its component functions XiX^iXi.

Space of Vector Fields: We denote by X(M)\mathfrak{X}(M)X(M) or Γ∞(TM)\Gamma^{\infty}(TM)Γ∞(TM) the space of all smooth vector fields on MMM. This space is both a vector space over R\mathbb{R}R and a module over the ring C∞(M)C^\infty(M)C∞(M) of smooth functions on MMM.

Exterior Algebra, Differential Forms

Note: we discuss multilinear functions over R\mathbb{R}R-vector spaces, but the theory can be generalized to C\mathbb{C}C-vector spaces or other vector spaces over a field.

Dual Space

Let V,WV,WV,W be a R\mathbb{R}R-vector space, we denote all of the mapping from VVV to WWW as Hom(V,W)\text{Hom}(V,W)Hom(V,W).

The dual space of VVV, denoted as V∨V^{\vee}V∨ or V∗V^*V∗, is the vector space of all linear maps from VVV to R\mathbb{R}R, i.e., V∗=Hom(V,R)V^* = \text{Hom}(V, \mathbb{R})V∗=Hom(V,R).

The elements of the dual space V∗V^*V∗ are called covectors or dual vectors.

The basis of the dual space V∗V^*V∗ is defined as the set of linear functionals that map each basis vector of VVV to R\mathbb{R}R, while mapping all other basis vectors to zero.

To be specific, let VVV be nnn-dimensional vector space, if {e1,e2,…,en}\{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n\}{e1​,e2​,…,en​} is a basis of VVV, then the dual basis {α1,α2,…,αn}\{\alpha^1, \alpha^2, \ldots, \alpha^n\}{α1,α2,…,αn} of V∗V^*V∗ is defined by:

αi(ej)=δji\alpha^i(e_j) = \delta^i_j αi(ej​)=δji​

where δji\delta^i_jδji​ is the Kronecker delta.

To show that {α1,α2,…,αn}\{\alpha^1, \alpha^2, \ldots, \alpha^n\}{α1,α2,…,αn} is a basis of V∗V^*V∗, we need to show that it is linearly independent and spans V∗V^*V∗.

Let f∈V∗f \in V^*f∈V∗ be a linear functional. We can express fff in terms of the dual basis as:

f=∑i=1nf(ei)αif = \sum_{i=1}^n f(e_i) \alpha^i f=i=1∑n​f(ei​)αi

where f(ei)f(e_i)f(ei​) are the coefficients of the linear functional fff in the dual basis. This shows that {α1,α2,…,αn}\{\alpha^1, \alpha^2, \ldots, \alpha^n\}{α1,α2,…,αn} spans V∗V^*V∗.

If ∑i=1nciαi=0\sum_{i=1}^n c_i \alpha^i = 0∑i=1n​ci​αi=0 for some coefficients cic_ici​, then for any basis vector ej∈Ve_j \in Vej​∈V, we have:

∑i=1nciαi(ej)=∑i=1nciδji=cj=0\sum_{i=1}^n c_i \alpha^i(e_j) = \sum_{i=1}^n c_i \delta^i_j = c_j = 0 i=1∑n​ci​αi(ej​)=i=1∑n​ci​δji​=cj​=0

for all j=1,2,…,nj = 1, 2, \ldots, nj=1,2,…,n. This implies that all coefficients ci=0c_i = 0ci​=0, showing that the dual basis is linearly independent.

Multilinear function

Let VkV^kVk be the kkk-fold Cartesian product of a vector space VVV, i.e., Vk=V×V×…×V⏟k timesV^k = \underbrace{V \times V \times \ldots \times V}_{k\text{ times}}Vk=k timesV×V×…×V​​ . A multilinear function or kkk-linear function is a function f:Vk→Rf: V^k \to \mathbb{R}f:Vk→R that is linear in each argument separately. It is a type (0,k)(0,k)(0,k) tensor.

One can prove that all multilinear functions on VVV forms a vector space, denoted as Lk(V)L_k(V)Lk​(V) or Mult(Vk;R)\text{Mult}(V^k; \mathbb{R})Mult(Vk;R).

Examples

  1. Dot Product: For a R\mathbb{R}R-vector space VVV with a standard basis {e1,e2,…,en}\{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n\}{e1​,e2​,…,en​}, the dot product is a bilinear function f:V2→Rf: V^2 \to \mathbb{R}f:V2→R defined as:

    f(x,y)=∑i=1nxiyif(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^n x^i y^i f(x,y)=i=1∑n​xiyi

    where x=∑i=1nxieix = \sum_{i=1}^n x^i e_ix=∑i=1n​xiei​ and y=∑i=1nyieiy = \sum_{i=1}^n y^i e_iy=∑i=1n​yiei​ are vectors in VVV.

  2. Determinant: The determinant is a multilinear function f:Vn→Rf: V^n \to \mathbb{R}f:Vn→R defined on the space of n×nn \times nn×n matrices. It is linear in each row (or column) of the matrix.

Let σ∈Sk\sigma\in S_kσ∈Sk​ be a permutation of the indices {1,2,…,k}\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}{1,2,…,k}. The left action of σ\sigmaσ on a multilinear function f:Vk→Rf: V^k \to \mathbb{R}f:Vk→R is defined as:

σf(v1,v2,…,vk)=f(vσ(1),vσ(2),…,vσ(k))\sigma f(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k) = f(v_{\sigma(1)}, v_{\sigma(2)}, \ldots, v_{\sigma(k)}) σf(v1​,v2​,…,vk​)=f(vσ(1)​,vσ(2)​,…,vσ(k)​)

A symmetric multilinear function is a multilinear function that is invariant under any permutation of its arguments.

Formally, σf=f\sigma f = fσf=f for any permutation σ∈Sk\sigma\in S_kσ∈Sk​.

An alternating multilinear function is a multilinear function that changes sign when two of its arguments are swapped, or equivalently, changes by a factor of sgn(σ)sgn(\sigma)sgn(σ) when permuted by σ\sigmaσ.

Formally, σf=sgn(σ)f\sigma f = \text{sgn}(\sigma) fσf=sgn(σ)f for any permutation σ∈Sk\sigma\in S_kσ∈Sk​, where sgn(σ)\text{sgn}(\sigma)sgn(σ) is the sign of the permutation.

All symmetric multilinear functions on VVV forms a vector space, denoted as Sk(V)S_k(V)Sk​(V) or Sym(Vk;R)\text{Sym}(V^k; \mathbb{R})Sym(Vk;R).
All alternating multilinear functions on VVV forms a vector space, denoted as Ak(V)A_k(V)Ak​(V) or Alt(Vk;R)\text{Alt}(V^k; \mathbb{R})Alt(Vk;R).

Symmetrizing and Alternating Operators

Let VVV be a vector space and f:Vk→Rf: V^k \to \mathbb{R}f:Vk→R be a multilinear function. We can define the symmetrizing operator and alternating operator on fff.

The symmetrizing operator S:Lk(V)→Sk(V)S: L_k(V) \to S_k(V)S:Lk​(V)→Sk​(V) is defined as:

S(f)(v1,v2,…,vk)=1k!∑σ∈SkσfS(f)(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k) = \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{\sigma \in S_k} \sigma f S(f)(v1​,v2​,…,vk​)=k!1​σ∈Sk​∑​σf

The symmetrizing operator takes a multilinear function and produces a symmetric multilinear function by averaging over all permutations of its arguments.

The alternating operator A:Lk(V)→Ak(V)A: L_k(V) \to A_k(V)A:Lk​(V)→Ak​(V) is defined as:

A(f)(v1,v2,…,vk)=1k!∑σ∈Sksgn(σ)σfA(f)(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k) = \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{\sigma \in S_k} \text{sgn}(\sigma) \sigma f A(f)(v1​,v2​,…,vk​)=k!1​σ∈Sk​∑​sgn(σ)σf

The alternating operator takes a multilinear function and produces an alternating multilinear function by summing over all permutations of its arguments, weighted by the sign of the permutation.

We shall only prove the alternating operator does produce an alternating multilinear function. The proof for the symmetrizing operator is trivial.

Proof: Let fff be a multilinear function and A(f)A(f)A(f) be the result of the alternating operator. For any permutation τ∈Sk\tau \in S_kτ∈Sk​, we have:

τA(f)=1k!∑σ∈Sksgn(σ)τ∘σf=1k!∑σ∈Sksgn(τ)sgn(τ∘σ)τ∘σf=1k!∑σ′∈Sksgn(σ′)σ′f=sgn(τ)A(f)\begin{align*}\tau A(f) &= \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{\sigma \in S_k} \text{sgn}(\sigma) \tau \circ\sigma f \\&= \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{\sigma \in S_k} \text{sgn}(\tau) \text{sgn}(\tau \circ \sigma) \tau \circ\sigma f \\&= \frac{1}{k!} \sum_{\sigma' \in S_k} \text{sgn}(\sigma') \sigma' f \\&= \text{sgn}(\tau) A(f)\end{align*} τA(f)​=k!1​σ∈Sk​∑​sgn(σ)τ∘σf=k!1​σ∈Sk​∑​sgn(τ)sgn(τ∘σ)τ∘σf=k!1​σ′∈Sk​∑​sgn(σ′)σ′f=sgn(τ)A(f)​

The alternating operator AAA satisfies the following properties:

  1. Linearity: For f1,f2∈Lk(V)f_1, f_2 \in L_k(V)f1​,f2​∈Lk​(V) and r,s∈Rr,s\in \mathbb{R}r,s∈R, we have:

    A(rf1+sf2)=rA(f1)+sA(f2)A(r f_1 + s f_2) = r A(f_1) + s A(f_2) A(rf1​+sf2​)=rA(f1​)+sA(f2​)

  2. A(f⊗A(g))=A(A(f)⊗g)=A(f⊗g)A(f\otimes A(g)) = A(A(f)\otimes g) = A(f\otimes g)A(f⊗A(g))=A(A(f)⊗g)=A(f⊗g)
  3. A∘A=AA\circ A = AA∘A=A

Tensor Product

Let f∈Lk(V)f\in L_k(V)f∈Lk​(V) and g∈Ll(V)g\in L_l(V)g∈Ll​(V) be two multilinear functions on VVV. The tensor product of fff and ggg, denoted as f⊗gf \otimes gf⊗g, is a multilinear function on Vk+lV^{k+l}Vk+l defined as:

(f⊗g)(v1,v2,…,vk+l)=f(v1,v2,…,vk)g(vk+1,vk+2,…,vk+l)(f \otimes g)(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_{k+l}) = f(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k) g(v_{k+1}, v_{k+2}, \ldots, v_{k+l}) (f⊗g)(v1​,v2​,…,vk+l​)=f(v1​,v2​,…,vk​)g(vk+1​,vk+2​,…,vk+l​)

The tensor product satisfies associativity and bilinearity.
Formally, we have:

  1. Associativity: (f⊗g)⊗h=f⊗(g⊗h)(f \otimes g) \otimes h = f \otimes (g \otimes h)(f⊗g)⊗h=f⊗(g⊗h) for any multilinear function h∈Lm(V)h \in L_m(V)h∈Lm​(V).
  2. Bilinearity: For f,f1,f2∈Lk(V)f,f_1,f_2 \in L_k(V)f,f1​,f2​∈Lk​(V) and g,g1,g2∈Ll(V)g,g_1,g_2 \in L_l(V)g,g1​,g2​∈Ll​(V); r,s∈Rr,s\in \mathbb Rr,s∈R, we have:

    (rf1+sf2)⊗g=r(f1⊗g)+s(f2⊗g)(r f_1 + s f_2) \otimes g = r (f_1 \otimes g) + s (f_2 \otimes g) (rf1​+sf2​)⊗g=r(f1​⊗g)+s(f2​⊗g)

    f⊗(rg1+sg2)=r(f⊗g1)+s(f⊗g2)f \otimes (r g_1 + s g_2) = r (f \otimes g_1) + s (f \otimes g_2) f⊗(rg1​+sg2​)=r(f⊗g1​)+s(f⊗g2​)

Example: Let (e1,…,en)(e_1,\ldots,e_n)(e1​,…,en​) be a basis of VVV, (α1,…,αn)(\alpha^1,\ldots,\alpha^n)(α1,…,αn) be the dual basis of V∗V^*V∗. Then the inner product is a bilinear function f:V×V→Rf: V\times V \to \mathbb{R}f:V×V→R defined as:

⟨x,y⟩=∑i=1nxiyi\langle x,y\rangle = \sum_{i=1}^n x^i y^i ⟨x,y⟩=i=1∑n​xiyi

where x=∑i=1nxieix = \sum_{i=1}^n x^i e_ix=∑i=1n​xiei​ and y=∑i=1nyieiy = \sum_{i=1}^n y^i e_iy=∑i=1n​yiei​ are vectors in VVV.

Now, let gij=⟨ei,ej⟩g_{ij}=\langle e_i,e_j\ranglegij​=⟨ei​,ej​⟩, ⟨x,y⟩=∑xiyjgij=∑αi(x)αj(y)gij=∑i,jgij(αi⊗αj)(x,y)\langle x,y\rangle = \sum x^i y^j g_{ij} = \sum \alpha^i(x) \alpha^j(y) g_{ij} = \sum_{i,j} g_{ij} (\alpha^i \otimes \alpha^j)(x,y)⟨x,y⟩=∑xiyjgij​=∑αi(x)αj(y)gij​=∑i,j​gij​(αi⊗αj)(x,y).

So we can write the inner product as a tensor product of the dual basis:

⟨x,y⟩=∑i,jgij(αi⊗αj)(x,y)\langle x,y\rangle = \sum_{i,j} g_{ij} (\alpha^i \otimes \alpha^j)(x,y) ⟨x,y⟩=i,j∑​gij​(αi⊗αj)(x,y)

Wedge Product

The wedge product or exterior product is an operation on alternating multilinear functions that produces a new alternating multilinear function. It is denoted by ∧\wedge∧ and is defined as follows:

For all f∈Ak(V)f \in A_k(V)f∈Ak​(V) and g∈Al(V)g \in A_l(V)g∈Al​(V), the wedge product f∧g∈Ak+l(V)f \wedge g \in A_{k+l}(V)f∧g∈Ak+l​(V) is defined by:

(f∧g)=(k+lk)A(f⊗g)(f \wedge g) = \binom{k+l}{k} A(f\otimes g)(f∧g)=(kk+l​)A(f⊗g)

or equivalently,

(f∧g)(v1,v2,…,vk+l)=1k!l!∑σ∈Sk+lsgn(σ)f(vσ(1),vσ(2),…,vσ(k))g(vσ(k+1),vσ(k+2),…,vσ(k+l))(f \wedge g)(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_{k+l}) = \frac{1}{k!l!} \sum_{\sigma \in S_{k+l}} \text{sgn}(\sigma) f(v_{\sigma(1)}, v_{\sigma(2)}, \ldots, v_{\sigma(k)}) g(v_{\sigma(k+1)}, v_{\sigma(k+2)}, \ldots, v_{\sigma(k+l)}) (f∧g)(v1​,v2​,…,vk+l​)=k!l!1​σ∈Sk+l​∑​sgn(σ)f(vσ(1)​,vσ(2)​,…,vσ(k)​)g(vσ(k+1)​,vσ(k+2)​,…,vσ(k+l)​)

To avoid division by factorials, we can define the wedge product as sum over sorted permutations:

(f∧g)(v1,v2,…,vk+l)=∑σ∈Sk+lσ(1)<σ(2)<…<σ(k)σ(k+1)<σ(k+2)<…<σ(k+l)sgn(σ)f(vσ(1),vσ(2),…,vσ(k))g(vσ(k+1),vσ(k+2),…,vσ(k+l))\begin{equation*} (f \wedge g)(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_{k+l}) = \sum_{\substack{\sigma \in S_{k+l} \\ \sigma(1) < \sigma(2) < \ldots < \sigma(k) \\ \sigma(k+1) < \sigma(k+2) < \ldots < \sigma(k+l)}} \text{sgn}(\sigma) f(v_{\sigma(1)}, v_{\sigma(2)}, \ldots, v_{\sigma(k)}) g(v_{\sigma(k+1)}, v_{\sigma(k+2)}, \ldots, v_{\sigma(k+l)})\end{equation*}(f∧g)(v1​,v2​,…,vk+l​)=σ∈Sk+l​σ(1)<σ(2)<…<σ(k)σ(k+1)<σ(k+2)<…<σ(k+l)​∑​sgn(σ)f(vσ(1)​,vσ(2)​,…,vσ(k)​)g(vσ(k+1)​,vσ(k+2)​,…,vσ(k+l)​)​

Let f∈Ak(V)f \in A_k(V)f∈Ak​(V) and g∈Al(V)g \in A_l(V)g∈Al​(V) be alternating multilinear functions. The wedge product satisfies the following properties:

  1. Bilinearity: It is bilinear on fff and ggg.
  2. Associativity: It is associative, i.e., (f∧g)∧h=f∧(g∧h)(f \wedge g) \wedge h = f \wedge (g \wedge h)(f∧g)∧h=f∧(g∧h) for any alternating multilinear function hhh.
  3. Anticommutativity: It is anticommutative, i.e., f∧g=(−1)klg∧ff \wedge g = (-1)^{kl} g \wedge ff∧g=(−1)klg∧f. Particularly, f∧f=0f \wedge f = 0f∧f=0 when kkk is odd.
  4. c∧f=cfc\wedge f=cfc∧f=cf, ∀c∈R\forall c\in \mathbb R∀c∈R.

Let r∈Nr\in Nr∈N, fi∈Aki(V)f_i\in A_{k_i}(V)fi​∈Aki​​(V) for i=1,2,…,ri=1,2,\ldots,ri=1,2,…,r, the wedge product f1∧f2∧…∧frf_1 \wedge f_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge f_rf1​∧f2​∧…∧fr​ is (k1+…+krk1,…,kr)A(f1⊗f2⊗…⊗fr)\binom{k_1+\ldots+k_r}{k_1,\ldots,k_r} A(f_1\otimes f_2\otimes \ldots \otimes f_r)(k1​,…,kr​k1​+…+kr​​)A(f1​⊗f2​⊗…⊗fr​), where (k1+…+krk1,…,kr)\binom{k_1+\ldots+k_r}{k_1,\ldots,k_r}(k1​,…,kr​k1​+…+kr​​) is the multinomial coefficient.

or equivalently,

(f1∧f2∧…∧fr)(v1,v2,…,vk1+…+kr)=1k1!k2!…kr!∑σ∈Sk1+…+krsgn(σ)f1(vσ(1),vσ(2),…,vσ(k1))f2(vσ(k1+1),vσ(k1+2),…,vσ(k1+k2))…fr(vσ(k1+…+kr−1+1),vσ(k1+…+kr−1+2),…,vσ(k1+…+kr))(f_1 \wedge f_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge f_r)(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_{k_1+\ldots+k_r}) = \frac{1}{k_1!k_2!\ldots k_r!} \sum_{\sigma \in S_{k_1+\ldots+k_r}} \text{sgn}(\sigma) f_1(v_{\sigma(1)}, v_{\sigma(2)}, \ldots, v_{\sigma(k_1)}) f_2(v_{\sigma(k_1+1)}, v_{\sigma(k_1+2)}, \ldots, v_{\sigma(k_1+k_2)}) \ldots f_r(v_{\sigma(k_1+\ldots+k_{r-1}+1)}, v_{\sigma(k_1+\ldots+k_{r-1}+2)}, \ldots, v_{\sigma(k_1+\ldots+k_r)}) (f1​∧f2​∧…∧fr​)(v1​,v2​,…,vk1​+…+kr​​)=k1​!k2​!…kr​!1​σ∈Sk1​+…+kr​​∑​sgn(σ)f1​(vσ(1)​,vσ(2)​,…,vσ(k1​)​)f2​(vσ(k1​+1)​,vσ(k1​+2)​,…,vσ(k1​+k2​)​)…fr​(vσ(k1​+…+kr−1​+1)​,vσ(k1​+…+kr−1​+2)​,…,vσ(k1​+…+kr​)​)

This shows the associativity directly.

Relation to determinant

Let α1,…,αn∈V∗\alpha^1,\ldots,\alpha^n\in V^*α1,…,αn∈V∗, v1,…,vn∈Vv_1,\ldots,v_n\in Vv1​,…,vn​∈V, then:

(α1∧α2∧…∧αn)(v1,v2,…,vn)=det⁡[(αi(vj))i,j](\alpha^1 \wedge \alpha^2 \wedge \ldots \wedge \alpha^n)(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n) = \det[(\alpha^i(v_j))_{i,j}] (α1∧α2∧…∧αn)(v1​,v2​,…,vn​)=det[(αi(vj​))i,j​]

This is the determinant of the matrix formed by evaluating the dual basis vectors on the vectors v1,v2,…,vnv_1, v_2, \ldots, v_nv1​,v2​,…,vn​.

Graded Algebra and Exterior Algebra

Graded Algebra

Let K\mathbb KK be a field, the graded algebra (A,×)(A,\times)(A,×) is a vector space AAA over K\mathbb KK equipped with a direct sum decomposition A=⨁k=0∞AkA = \bigoplus_{k=0}^{\infty} A_kA=⨁k=0∞​Ak​, where each AkA_kAk​ is a vector space of degree kkk. The multiplication operation ×:A×A→A\times: A \times A \to A×:A×A→A satisfies:

×:Ak×Al→Ak+l\times: A_k \times A_l \to A_{k+l} ×:Ak​×Al​→Ak+l​

(A,×)(A,\times)(A,×) is called anticommutative if for all a∈Aka \in A_ka∈Ak​ and b∈Alb \in A_lb∈Al​, ab=(−1)klbaab = (-1)^{kl} baab=(−1)klba.

A homomorphism of graded algebra is an algebra homomorphism that preserves the degree.

Exterior Algebra

The exterior algebra or Grassmann algebra (⋀V,∧)(\bigwedge V, \wedge)(⋀V,∧) is a anticommutative graded algebra defined on a vector space VVV with the wedge product ∧\wedge∧, where ⋀V=⨁k=0∞Ak(V)\bigwedge V = \bigoplus_{k=0}^{\infty} A_k(V)⋀V=⨁k=0∞​Ak​(V). When VVV is finite-dimensional, we can write ⋀V=⨁k=0dim⁡VAk(V)\bigwedge V = \bigoplus_{k=0}^{\dim V} A_k(V)⋀V=⨁k=0dimV​Ak​(V) or ⋀V=⨁k=0n⋀kV\bigwedge V = \bigoplus_{k=0}^{n} \bigwedge ^k V⋀V=⨁k=0n​⋀kV, where n=dim⁡Vn = \dim Vn=dimV.

This is because Ak(V)=0A_k(V) = 0Ak​(V)=0 for k>dim⁡Vk > \dim Vk>dimV, which we shall prove later.

⋀kV\bigwedge^k V⋀kV is called the kkk-th exterior power of VVV.

Basis of LkL_kLk​

Let I=(i1,i2,…,ik)I=(i_1,i_2,\ldots,i_k)I=(i1​,i2​,…,ik​) be a kkk-indices, e1,…,ene_1,\ldots,e_ne1​,…,en​ and α1,…,αn\alpha^1,\ldots,\alpha^nα1,…,αn are the basis of the vector space VVV and its dual V∗V^*V∗ respectively. Denote (ei1,ei2,…,eik)(e_{i_1}, e_{i_2}, \ldots, e_{i_k})(ei1​​,ei2​​,…,eik​​) as eIe_IeI​, and αI=αi1∧αi2∧…∧αik\alpha^I = \alpha^{i_1} \wedge \alpha^{i_2} \wedge \ldots \wedge \alpha^{i_k}αI=αi1​∧αi2​∧…∧αik​. αI(eJ)=δJI\alpha^I(e_J) = \delta^I_JαI(eJ​)=δJI​, where δJI\delta^I_JδJI​ is the generalized Kronecker delta, which is 111 if I=JI=JI=J and 000 otherwise.

Let I=(1≤i1<⋯<ik≤n)I=(1 \leq i_1 < \cdots < i_k \leq n)I=(1≤i1​<⋯<ik​≤n), we claim that {αI}I\{\alpha^I\}_{I}{αI}I​ is a basis of Ak(V)A_k(V)Ak​(V).

The proof is similar to the proof in the dual space section.

Corollary 1: The dimension of Ak(V)A_k(V)Ak​(V) is (nk)\binom{n}{k}(kn​), where n=dim⁡Vn = \dim Vn=dimV.

Corollary 2: The dimension of ⋀V\bigwedge V⋀V is ∑k=0n(nk)=2n\sum_{k=0}^{n} \binom{n}{k} = 2^n∑k=0n​(kn​)=2n, where n=dim⁡Vn = \dim Vn=dimV.

Corollary 3: The dimension of Ak(V)A_k(V)Ak​(V) is 000 for k>dim⁡Vk > \dim Vk>dimV.

Proof: For k>dim⁡Vk > \dim Vk>dimV, the set of indices I=(i1,i2,…,ik)I=(i_1,i_2,\ldots,i_k)I=(i1​,i2​,…,ik​) cannot be chosen such that 1≤i1<i2<…<ik≤n1 \leq i_1 < i_2 < \ldots < i_k \leq n1≤i1​<i2​<…<ik​≤n, hence Ak(V)=0A_k(V) = 0Ak​(V)=0.

Inner Product

Let VVV be a vector space, v∈Vv\in Vv∈V, and ω∈⋀kV\omega \in \bigwedge^k Vω∈⋀kV. The inner product of vvv and ω\omegaω, denoted as ιvω\iota_v \omegaιv​ω, is a linear map ιv:⋀kV→⋀k−1V\iota_v: \bigwedge^k V \to \bigwedge^{k-1} Vιv​:⋀kV→⋀k−1V defined by:

ιv(ω)=∑i=1k(−1)i−1αi(v)α1∧…∧αi^∧…∧αk\iota_v(\omega) = \sum_{i=1}^k (-1)^{i-1} \alpha^i(v) \alpha^{1} \wedge \ldots \wedge \widehat{\alpha^i} \wedge \ldots \wedge \alpha^{k} ιv​(ω)=i=1∑k​(−1)i−1αi(v)α1∧…∧αi∧…∧αk

where αi^\widehat{\alpha^i}αi means that the iii-th term is omitted from the wedge product.

The inner product ιv\iota_vιv​ is called the interior product or contraction with respect to the vector vvv. It reduces the degree of the form by 1.

Let α∈⋀kV,β∈⋀lV\alpha \in \bigwedge^k V, \beta \in \bigwedge^l Vα∈⋀kV,β∈⋀lV, the inner product satisfies the following properties:

  1. Nilpotency: ιvιvα=0\iota_v \iota_v \alpha = 0ιv​ιv​α=0 for any v∈Vv \in Vv∈V.
  2. ιv(α∧β)=ιv(α)∧β+(−1)kα∧ιv(β)\iota_v(\alpha \wedge \beta) = \iota_v(\alpha) \wedge \beta + (-1)^k \alpha \wedge \iota_v(\beta)ιv​(α∧β)=ιv​(α)∧β+(−1)kα∧ιv​(β).
  3. Linearity: ιav+bw(α)=aιv(α)+bιw(α)\iota_{av + bw}(\alpha) = a \iota_v(\alpha) + b \iota_w(\alpha)ιav+bw​(α)=aιv​(α)+bιw​(α) for any a,b∈Ra,b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R and v,w∈Vv,w \in Vv,w∈V.

Pullback

Let L:W→VL: W \to VL:W→V be a linear map between vector spaces WWW and VVV. The pullback of exterior forms under LLL, denoted as L∗:⋀kV→⋀kWL^*: \bigwedge^k V \to \bigwedge^k WL∗:⋀kV→⋀kW, is defined by:

L∗(ω)(w1,w2,…,wk)=ω(L(w1),L(w2),…,L(wk))L^*(\omega)(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k) = \omega(L(w_1), L(w_2), \ldots, L(w_k)) L∗(ω)(w1​,w2​,…,wk​)=ω(L(w1​),L(w2​),…,L(wk​))

for all w1,w2,…,wk∈W∗w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_k \in W^*w1​,w2​,…,wk​∈W∗.

The pullback satisfies the following properties:

  1. L∗(α∧β)=L∗(α)∧L∗(β)L^*(\alpha \wedge \beta) = L^*(\alpha) \wedge L^*(\beta)L∗(α∧β)=L∗(α)∧L∗(β)
  2. ιw(L∗(α))=L∗(ιL(w)(α))\iota_w(L^*(\alpha)) = L^*(\iota_{L(w)}(\alpha))ιw​(L∗(α))=L∗(ιL(w)​(α)).

Differential Forms

We have defined the exterior algebra ⋀V\bigwedge V⋀V and the wedge product ∧\wedge∧ on an abstract vector space VVV. Now we can define differential forms on a manifold MMM, where VVV is the tangent space TpMT_pMTp​M at a point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M.

Cotangent Space and Cotangent Bundle

The cotangent space Tp∗MT^*_pMTp∗​M at a point p∈Mp \in Mp∈M is the dual space of the tangent space TpMT_pMTp​M. It consists of all linear functionals on TpMT_pMTp​M. Elements of the cotangent space are called covectors or differential 1-forms.

The cotangent space Tp∗MT^*_pMTp∗​M is a vector space of dimension nnn, where nnn is the dimension of the manifold MMM.

The cotangent bundle T∗MT^*MT∗M is the disjoint union of all cotangent spaces at each point in MMM:

T∗M=⋃p∈MTp∗M={(p,ωp):p∈M,ωp∈Tp∗M}T^*M = \bigcup_{p \in M} T^*_pM = \{(p, \omega_p) : p \in M, \omega_p \in T^*_pM\} T∗M=p∈M⋃​Tp∗​M={(p,ωp​):p∈M,ωp​∈Tp∗​M}

The cotangent bundle T∗MT^*MT∗M is a manifold of dimension 2n2n2n.

Local coordinates on the cotangent bundle can be defined as (x1,x2,…,xn,ω1,ω2,…,ωn)(x^1, x^2, \ldots, x^n, \omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_n)(x1,x2,…,xn,ω1​,ω2​,…,ωn​), where (x1,x2,…,xn)(x^1, x^2, \ldots, x^n)(x1,x2,…,xn) are local coordinates on MMM and (ω1,ω2,…,ωn)(\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots, \omega_n)(ω1​,ω2​,…,ωn​) are the components of a covector in the cotangent space with respect to the dual basis {dx1,dx2,…,dxn}\{dx^1, dx^2, \ldots, dx^n\}{dx1,dx2,…,dxn}.

Differential kkk-Forms

Let MMM be a smooth manifold of dimension nnn. A differential kkk-form on MMM is a smooth section of the kkk-th exterior power of the cotangent bundle, denoted as ⋀kT∗M\bigwedge^k T^*M⋀kT∗M.

In other words, a differential kkk-form on a open subset U⊆MU \subseteq MU⊆M is a mapping $$ \omega: U \to \bigwedge^k T^*M $$ that assigns to each point p∈Up \in Up∈U an alternating multilinear function ω(p):TpMk→R\omega(p): T_pM^k \to \mathbb{R}ω(p):Tp​Mk→R and ω∈Ak(TpM)\omega \in A_k(T_pM)ω∈Ak​(Tp​M) for all p∈Up \in Up∈U.

Let {e1=∂∂x1∣p,e2=∂∂x2∣p,…,en=∂∂xn∣p}\{e_1 = \left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^1}\right|_p, e_2 = \left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^2}\right|_p, \ldots, e_n = \left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^n}\right|_p\}{e1​=∂x1∂​​p​,e2​=∂x2∂​​p​,…,en​=∂xn∂​​p​} be a basis of TpMT_pMTp​M. The corresponding dual basis of Tp∗MT^*_pMTp∗​M is {dx1,dx2,…,dxn}\{dx^1, dx^2, \ldots, dx^n\}{dx1,dx2,…,dxn}, where dxi(ej)=δjidx^i(e_j) = \delta^i_jdxi(ej​)=δji​.

A basis of Ak(TpM)A_k(T_pM)Ak​(Tp​M) is then given by {dxI}I\{dx^I\}_{I}{dxI}I​, where I=(i1,i2,…,ik)I = (i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_k)I=(i1​,i2​,…,ik​) is a kkk-tuple of indices with 1≤i1<i2<…<ik≤n1 \leq i_1 < i_2 < \ldots < i_k \leq n1≤i1​<i2​<…<ik​≤n, and dxIdx^IdxI denotes the wedge product dxi1∧dxi2∧…∧dxikdx^{i_1} \wedge dx^{i_2} \wedge \ldots \wedge dx^{i_k}dxi1​∧dxi2​∧…∧dxik​.

Therefore, a differential kkk-form ω\omegaω can be expressed in local coordinates as:

ω=∑IfIdxI\omega = \sum_{I} f_I dx^I ω=I∑​fI​dxI

where fI:U→Rf_I: U \to \mathbb{R}fI​:U→R are functions on UUU.

A differential kkk-form ω\omegaω is called smooth if all the functions fIf_IfI​ are smooth functions on UUU.

Let Ωk(M)=Γ∞(⋀kT∗M)\Omega^k(M)=\Gamma^{\infty}(\bigwedge^k T^*M)Ωk(M)=Γ∞(⋀kT∗M) be the space of all smooth differential kkk-forms on a manifold MMM. It is a vector space over R\mathbb{R}R.

The wedge product of two differential forms ω1∈Ωk(M)\omega_1 \in \Omega^k(M)ω1​∈Ωk(M) and ω2∈Ωl(M)\omega_2 \in \Omega^l(M)ω2​∈Ωl(M) is pointwise defined as:

(ω1∧ω2)(p)=ω1(p)∧ω2(p)=∑I,JfI(p)gJ(p)dxI∧dxJ(\omega_1 \wedge \omega_2)(p) = \omega_1(p) \wedge \omega_2(p) = \sum_{I,J} f_I(p) g_J(p) dx^I \wedge dx^J (ω1​∧ω2​)(p)=ω1​(p)∧ω2​(p)=I,J∑​fI​(p)gJ​(p)dxI∧dxJ

where fIf_IfI​ and gJg_JgJ​ are the coefficients of ω1\omega_1ω1​ and ω2\omega_2ω2​ in local coordinates, respectively. Note that if III and JJJ are such that I∩J≠∅I \cap J \neq \emptysetI∩J=∅, then dxI∧dxJ=0dx^I \wedge dx^J = 0dxI∧dxJ=0 due to the anticommutativity of the wedge product.
The wedge product of differential forms is bilinear and associative, and it satisfies the anticommutativity property:

ω1∧ω2=(−1)klω2∧ω1\omega_1 \wedge \omega_2 = (-1)^{kl} \omega_2 \wedge \omega_1 ω1​∧ω2​=(−1)klω2​∧ω1​

Let Ω∗(M)=⨁k=0nΩk(M)\Omega^*(M) = \bigoplus_{k=0}^n \Omega^k(M)Ω∗(M)=⨁k=0n​Ωk(M) be the space of all smooth differential forms on MMM. It is a graded algebra with respect to the wedge product ∧\wedge∧.

Coordinate Functions and Their Differentials

Let (U,x)=(U,x1,…,xn)(U, x) = (U, x^1, \ldots, x^n)(U,x)=(U,x1,…,xn) be a coordinate chart on a smooth manifold MMM of dimension nnn. The coordinate functions xi:U→Rx^i: U \to \mathbb{R}xi:U→R are smooth functions that assign to each point p∈Up \in Up∈U its iii-th coordinate xi(p)x^i(p)xi(p).

The coordinate 1-forms dxidx^idxi are smooth sections of the cotangent bundle, each dxi:U→T∗Mdx^i: U \to T^*Mdxi:U→T∗M satisfies $$\pi \circ dx^i = \text{id}_U$$
where π:T∗M→M\pi: T^*M \to Mπ:T∗M→M is the bundle projection.

We have dxi∣p:TpM→Rdx^i|_p: T_pM \to \mathbb{R}dxi∣p​:Tp​M→R defined by dxi∣p(v)=v(xi(p))dx^i|_p(v) = v(x^i(p))dxi∣p​(v)=v(xi(p)) for any tangent vector v∈TpMv \in T_pMv∈Tp​M. The coordinate 1-forms satisfy the dual basis property: $$\left.dx^i\right|_p\left(\left.\frac{\partial}{\partial x^j}\right|_p\right) = \delta^i_j$$
making {dx1∣p,…,dxn∣p}\{dx^1|_p, \ldots, dx^n|_p\}{dx1∣p​,…,dxn∣p​} the dual basis of Tp∗MT_p^*MTp∗​M corresponding to the coordinate basis {∂∂x1∣p,…,∂∂xn∣p}\{\frac{\partial}{\partial x^1}|_p, \ldots, \frac{\partial}{\partial x^n}|_p\}{∂x1∂​∣p​,…,∂xn∂​∣p​} of TpMT_pMTp​M.

Any differential kkk-form on UUU can be uniquely expressed as ω=∑∣I∣=kfI dxI\omega = \sum_{|I|=k} f_I \, dx^Iω=∑∣I∣=k​fI​dxI, where I=(i1,…,ik)I = (i_1, \ldots, i_k)I=(i1​,…,ik​) with 1≤i1<⋯<ik≤n1 \leq i_1 < \cdots < i_k \leq n1≤i1​<⋯<ik​≤n, dxI=dxi1∧⋯∧dxikdx^I = dx^{i_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^{i_k}dxI=dxi1​∧⋯∧dxik​, and fI:U→Rf_I: U \to \mathbb{R}fI​:U→R are smooth functions.

Pullback of Differential Forms

Let F:M→NF: M \to NF:M→N be a smooth map between manifolds, and let ω∈Ωk(N)\omega \in \Omega^k(N)ω∈Ωk(N) be a differential kkk-form on NNN. The pullback of ω\omegaω by FFF, denoted as F∗ωF^*\omegaF∗ω, is a differential kkk-form on MMM defined by:

(F∗ω)(p)(v1,v2,…,vk)=ω(F(p))(dFp(v1),dFp(v2),…,dFp(vk))(F^*\omega)(p)(v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k) = \omega(F(p))(dF_p(v_1), dF_p(v_2), \ldots, dF_p(v_k)) (F∗ω)(p)(v1​,v2​,…,vk​)=ω(F(p))(dFp​(v1​),dFp​(v2​),…,dFp​(vk​))

where dFp:TpM→TF(p)NdF_p: T_pM \to T_{F(p)}NdFp​:Tp​M→TF(p)​N is the differential of FFF at the point ppp.

Local Coordinate Expression

Let (U,x1,…,xm)(U, x^1, \ldots, x^m)(U,x1,…,xm) and (V,y1,…,yn)(V, y^1, \ldots, y^n)(V,y1,…,yn) be coordinate charts on MMM and NNN respectively, with F(U)⊆VF(U) \subseteq VF(U)⊆V.

Let F:M→NF: M \to NF:M→N have local coordinate representation Fi=yi∘F∘x−1F^i = y^i \circ F \circ x^{-1}Fi=yi∘F∘x−1, where (x1,…,xm)(x^1, \ldots, x^m)(x1,…,xm) are coordinates on MMM and (y1,…,yn)(y^1, \ldots, y^n)(y1,…,yn) are coordinates on NNN.

If ω\omegaω has the local expression:

ω=∑∣I∣=kfI dyI\omega = \sum_{|I|=k} f_I \, dy^I ω=∣I∣=k∑​fI​dyI

where fI:V→Rf_I: V \to \mathbb{R}fI​:V→R are smooth functions.

Then the pullback F∗ωF^*\omegaF∗ω has the expression:

F∗ω=∑∣I∣=k(fI∘F) F∗(dyI)=∑∣I∣=k(fI∘F) d(yi1∘F)∧⋯∧d(yik∘F)\begin{align*}F^*\omega &= \sum_{|I|=k} (f_I \circ F) \, F^*(dy^I) \\&= \sum_{|I|=k} (f_I \circ F) \, d(y^{i_1} \circ F) \wedge \cdots \wedge d(y^{i_k} \circ F) \end{align*}F∗ω​=∣I∣=k∑​(fI​∘F)F∗(dyI)=∣I∣=k∑​(fI​∘F)d(yi1​∘F)∧⋯∧d(yik​∘F)​

Note:

d(yij∘F)=d(Fij∘x)=∑l=1m∂Fij∂xldxld(y^{i_j} \circ F) = d(F^{i_j} \circ x) = \sum_{l=1}^m \frac{\partial F^{i_j}}{\partial x^l} dx^l d(yij​∘F)=d(Fij​∘x)=l=1∑m​∂xl∂Fij​​dxl

Therefore:

F∗ω=∑∣I∣=k(fI∘F) (∑l1=1m∂Fi1∂xl1dxl1)∧⋯∧(∑lk=1m∂Fik∂xlkdxlk)F^*\omega = \sum_{|I|=k} (f_I \circ F) \, \left(\sum_{l_1=1}^m \frac{\partial F^{i_1}}{\partial x^{l_1}} dx^{l_1}\right) \wedge \cdots \wedge \left(\sum_{l_k=1}^m \frac{\partial F^{i_k}}{\partial x^{l_k}} dx^{l_k}\right) F∗ω=∣I∣=k∑​(fI​∘F)(l1​=1∑m​∂xl1​∂Fi1​​dxl1​)∧⋯∧(lk​=1∑m​∂xlk​∂Fik​​dxlk​)

Properties of Pullback

The pullback operation satisfies the following important properties:

  1. Linearity: F∗(aω1+bω2)=aF∗ω1+bF∗ω2F^*(a\omega_1 + b\omega_2) = aF^*\omega_1 + bF^*\omega_2F∗(aω1​+bω2​)=aF∗ω1​+bF∗ω2​ for a,b∈Ra,b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R

  2. Preservation of wedge product:

    F∗(ω1∧ω2)=F∗ω1∧F∗ω2F^*(\omega_1 \wedge \omega_2) = F^*\omega_1 \wedge F^*\omega_2 F∗(ω1​∧ω2​)=F∗ω1​∧F∗ω2​

  3. Functoriality: If G:L→MG: L \to MG:L→M and F:M→NF: M \to NF:M→N are smooth maps, then:

    (F∘G)∗=G∗∘F∗(F \circ G)^* = G^* \circ F^* (F∘G)∗=G∗∘F∗

  4. Identity: (idM)∗=idΩ∗(M)(\text{id}_M)^* = \text{id}_{\Omega^*(M)}(idM​)∗=idΩ∗(M)​

Exterior Derivative

The Exterior Derivative is an operator d:Ωk(M)→Ωk+1(M)d: \Omega^k(M) \to \Omega^{k+1}(M)d:Ωk(M)→Ωk+1(M) that generalizes the concept of differentiation to differential forms. It is defined as follows:

dω=∑∣I∣=k(∑j=1n∂fI∂xj)dxj∧dxId\omega = \sum_{|I|=k} \left( \sum_{j=1}^n \frac{\partial f_I}{\partial x^j} \right) dx^j \wedge dx^Idω=∣I∣=k∑​(j=1∑n​∂xj∂fI​​)dxj∧dxI

where ω=∑∣I∣=kfI dxI\omega = \sum_{|I|=k} f_I \, dx^Iω=∑∣I∣=k​fI​dxI is a differential kkk-form on MMM, and dxI=dxi1∧⋯∧dxikdx^I = dx^{i_1} \wedge \cdots \wedge dx^{i_k}dxI=dxi1​∧⋯∧dxik​ for I=(i1,…,ik)I = (i_1, \ldots, i_k)I=(i1​,…,ik​).

It can be naturally extended to the exterior algebra ⋀∗(M)\bigwedge^*(M)⋀∗(M) as follows:

d(ω)=∑k=0ndωkd(\omega) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} d\omega_k d(ω)=k=0∑n​dωk​

where ω=∑k=0nωk\omega = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \omega_kω=∑k=0n​ωk​ and ωk∈Ωk(M)\omega_k \in \Omega^k(M)ωk​∈Ωk(M).

The exterior derivative satisfies the following properties:

  1. Linearity: d(aω1+bω2)=adω1+bdω2d(a\omega_1 + b\omega_2) = a d\omega_1 + b d\omega_2d(aω1​+bω2​)=adω1​+bdω2​ for a,b∈Ra,b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R.
  2. Nilpotency: d2=0d^2 = 0d2=0, i.e., d(dω)=0d(d\omega) = 0d(dω)=0 for any differential form ω\omegaω.
  3. Antiderivation Property: d(ω1∧ω2)=dω1∧ω2+(−1)kω1∧dω2d(\omega_1 \wedge \omega_2) = d\omega_1 \wedge \omega_2 + (-1)^k \omega_1 \wedge d\omega_2d(ω1​∧ω2​)=dω1​∧ω2​+(−1)kω1​∧dω2​ for ω1∈Ωk(M)\omega_1 \in \Omega^k(M)ω1​∈Ωk(M) and ω2∈Ωl(M)\omega_2 \in \Omega^l(M)ω2​∈Ωl(M).
  4. Pullback Compatibility: For any smooth map F:M→NF: M \to NF:M→N, we have:

    F∗(dω)=d(F∗ω)F^*(d\omega) = d(F^*\omega) F∗(dω)=d(F∗ω)

  5. Vanishing of Top Forms: dω=0d\omega = 0dω=0 if ω∈Ωn(M)\omega\in \Omega^n(M)ω∈Ωn(M), where n=dim⁡Mn = \dim Mn=dimM.

de Rham Theory

TODO.

Poincaré Lemma

TODO.

Integration of Differential Forms

TODO.

Stokes’ Theorem

TODO.

entry.title: GAN and Wasserstein GAN
entry.content:
Introduction

Generative Adversarial Networks is invented in 2014 by Ian J.Goodfellow, et. al. as a generative models, its idea derives from the game theory where two player compete against one another.

So they design an architecture that has two network where one is responible for generating fake data that is similar from the data and another one is responible for discriminating between fake and true data.

Mathematics Formulas

GAN is formulated as a min-max problem

min⁡Gmax⁡DV(D,G)V(D,G)=Ex∼pdata(x)[log⁡D(x)]+Ex∼pg(x)[log⁡(1−D(x))]\min_{G} \max_{D} V(D,G) \\V(D,G) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}(x)}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{g}(x)}[\log(1 - D(x))]Gmin​Dmax​V(D,G)V(D,G)=Ex∼pdata​(x)​[logD(x)]+Ex∼pg​(x)​[log(1−D(x))]

Where DDD is the discriminator, GGG is the generator, pdata(x)p_{data}(x)pdata​(x) is the distribution of the real data, and pg(x)p_{g}(x)pg​(x) is the distribution of the noise input to the generator.

V(D,G)V(D,G)V(D,G) can be interpreted as the value function that represents the “distance” between the real data distribution and the generated data distribution.

Network Architecture

The architecture of GAN consists of two main components:

  1. Generator (G):
  • Noise input zzz is sampled from a prior distribution (e.g., Gaussian or uniform).
  • Hidden layers transform the noise into a data sample.
  • Output layer generates a data sample (e.g., image).
  1. Discriminator (D):
  • Takes a data sample (either real or generated).
  • Hidden layers process the sample.
  • Output layer produces a probability score indicating whether the sample is real or fake.

Training Process

The training process of GAN involves alternating between training the discriminator and the generator:

  1. Train Discriminator:
  • Sample a batch of real data from the dataset with label 1.
  • Sample a batch of noise and generate fake data using the generator with label 0.
  • Update the discriminator to maximize its ability to distinguish real from fake data.
  1. Train Generator:
  • Sample a batch of noise and generate fake data using the generator.
  • Update the generator to minimize the discriminator’s ability to distinguish fake data from real data.
  1. Repeat:
  • Continue alternating between training the discriminator and the generator until convergence or for a fixed number of epochs

Merits and Limitations

Merits

  • High-Quality Samples: GANs can generate high-quality samples that are often indistinguishable from real data.
  • Versatile Applications: GANs can be applied to various domains, including image generation
  • Unsupervised Learning: GANs can learn from unlabeled data, making them suitable for unsupervised learning tasks.

Limitations

  • Training Instability: GANs can be difficult to train due to issues like mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and oscillations.
  • Sensitive to Hyperparameters: GANs require careful tuning of hyperparameters, such as learning rates and batch sizes, to achieve stable training.
  • Lack of Diversity: GANs may generate samples that lack diversity, especially if the training data is limited or biased.

Analysis

Mode Collapse

Mode collapse is a common issue in GAN training where the generator produces a limited variety of outputs, often focusing on a few modes of the data distribution. This can lead to a lack of diversity in generated samples.

This is because the manifold of data is not necessarily connected (MNIST), but the transformation from the noise space to the data space is continuous. So it either gives up generating some data or generates some data that is not actually in the data manifold. (In the vacant middle of two number manifold).

See AE-OT-GAN.

Training Difficulties

Training GANs can be challenging due to the adversarial nature of the training process. The more powerful the discriminator becomes, the harder it is for the generator to produce samples that fool the discriminator.

This is because the loss function has the problem of vanishing gradients when the discriminator is too good at its job.

Fix GGG, the optimal DDD is given by:

D∗(x)=pdata(x)pdata(x)+pg(x)D^*(x)=\frac{p_{data}(x)}{p_{data}(x)+p_{g}(x)}D∗(x)=pdata​(x)+pg​(x)pdata​(x)​

Now we observe the loss of GGG has become:

2JS(pdata∣∣pg)−2log⁡22JS(p_{data}||p_{g})-2\log 22JS(pdata​∣∣pg​)−2log2

But when the support of pdatap_{data}pdata​ and pgp_{g}pg​ has no intersection, the JS loss shall be constant. So the network cannot be trained since there is no meaningful information can be acquired.

Wasserstein GAN (WGAN)

Wasserstein GAN (WGAN) is a variant of GAN that addresses some of the limitations of the original GAN formulation, particularly the issues related to training stability and mode collapse. WGAN uses the Wasserstein distance (also known as Earth Mover’s Distance) to measure the distance between the real data distribution and the generated data distribution.

See this article for more about Wasserstein distance: Wasserstein Distance and Optimal Transport

The important properties of Wasserstein distance are:

  1. Continuity: The Wasserstein distance is continuous with respect to the distributions.
  2. Metric: The Wasserstein distance satisfies the triangle inequality and is a true metric.
  3. Independence of Support: When the supports of two distributions have no overlap, Wasserstein distance still provides a suitable gradient for the generator to update.

Formulation

The original Wasserstein is difficult to compute, so WGAN uses a simplified version of the 1-Wasserstein distance, which can be computed using the Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality:

W(pdata,pg)=sup⁡∥f∥L≤1Ex∼pdata[f(x)]−Ex∼pg[f(x)]W(p_{data}, p_{g}) = \sup_{\|f\|_L \leq 1} \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{g}}[f(x)]W(pdata​,pg​)=∥f∥L​≤1sup​Ex∼pdata​​[f(x)]−Ex∼pg​​[f(x)]

Where fff is a 1-Lipschitz function, ∥f∥L≤1\|f\|_L \leq 1∥f∥L​≤1.

Actually if fff is a K-Lipschitz function, just divide the result by KKK. So long as the function is finite Lipschitz, it can be used to estimate the Wasserstein distance.

Modification of GAN

The loss function for the discriminator (critic) is modified to use the Wasserstein distance instead of the original GAN loss. So the objective becomes:

max⁡DL=Ex∼pdata[D(x)]−Ex∼pg[D(x)]\max_{D} L = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{data}}[D(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{g}}[D(x)]Dmax​L=Ex∼pdata​​[D(x)]−Ex∼pg​​[D(x)]

The discriminator is now no longer trying to output probabilities, but rather to output a real-valued score that reflects the “realness” of the input data.

To ensure that DDD is a K-Lipschitz function, WGAN applies weight clipping to the discriminator’s weights. This means that the weights of the discriminator are constrained to lie within a certain range (e.g., [-0.01, 0.01]). So it must be limited to a finite Lipschitz constant.

For the generator, since the first term is not dependent on GGG, the objective becomes:

min⁡GL=−Ex∼pg[D(x)]\min_{G} L = -\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p_{g}}[D(x)]Gmin​L=−Ex∼pg​​[D(x)]

entry.title: Wasserstein Distance and Optimal Transport
entry.content:
Optimal Transport Problem

Definition

Given two probability measures μ\muμ and ν uν on measurable spaces (X,A)(X, \mathcal{A})(X,A) and (Y,B)(Y, \mathcal{B})(Y,B), respectively, the Optimal Transport Problem seeks to find a transport plan π\piπ that minimizes the cost of transporting mass from μ\muμ to ν uν. The cost function is typically defined as a function c:X×Y→Rc: X imes Y o \mathbb{R}c:X×Y→R, which quantifies the cost of moving a unit mass from point x∈Xx \in Xx∈X to point y∈Yy \in Yy∈Y.

The problem can be formulated as follows (Monge’s formulation):

inf⁡T∫Xc(x,T(x)) dμ(x) egin{aligned}\inf_{T} \int_{X} c(x, T(x)) \, d\mu(x)\end{aligned}Tinf​∫X​c(x,T(x))dμ(x)​

where T:X→YT: X o YT:X→Y is a transport map that pushes μ\muμ forward to ν uν, i.e., T∗μ=νT_* \mu = uT∗​μ=ν.

Such a transport map TTT is called an optimal transport map if it minimizes the cost function, although it is not always guaranteed to exist since the pushforward is not necessarily possible.

More generally, we can express the problem as (Kantorovich’s formulation):

inf⁡π∈Π(μ,ν)∫X×Yc(x,y) dπ(x,y) egin{aligned}\inf_{\pi \in \Pi(\mu, u)} \int_{X imes Y} c(x, y) \, d\pi(x, y)\end{aligned}π∈Π(μ,ν)inf​∫X×Y​c(x,y)dπ(x,y)​

where Π(μ,ν)\Pi(\mu, u)Π(μ,ν) is the set of all joint distributions of μ\muμ and ν uν.

Dual Formulation

The primal problem is a convex optimization problem since it is linear w.r.t. π\piπ and the feasible set is convex.

The constraint is that π\piπ must be a joint distribution of μ\muμ and ν uν, which can be expressed as:

∫X×Yf(x) dγ(x,y)=∫Xf(x) dμ(x),∀f∈C(X)∫X×Yg(y) dγ(x,y)=∫Yg(y) dν(y),∀g∈C(Y) egin{aligned}\int_{X imes Y} f(x) \, d\gamma(x, y) &= \int_X f(x) \, d\mu(x), \quad orall f \in C(X)\\int_{X imes Y} g(y) \, d\gamma(x, y) &= \int_Y g(y) \, d u(y), \quad orall g \in C(Y)\end{aligned}∫X×Y​f(x)dγ(x,y)∫X×Y​g(y)dγ(x,y)​=∫X​f(x)dμ(x),∀f∈C(X)=∫Y​g(y)dν(y),∀g∈C(Y)​

where C(X)C(X)C(X) and C(Y)C(Y)C(Y) are the spaces of continuous functions on XXX and YYY, respectively.

This form allows us to derive the dual formulation of the optimal transport problem, whose Lagrangian is given by:

L(π,λ,μ)=∫X×Yc(x,y)dπ+∫Xλ(x)(dμ−Proj⁡X(dπ))+∫Yμ(y)(dν−Proj⁡Y(dπ))=∫X×Y(c(x,y)−λ(x)−μ(y))dπ+∫Xλ(x)dμ+∫Yμ(y)dν egin{aligned}L(\pi, \lambda, \mu) &= \int_{X imes Y} c(x,y) d\pi + \int_{X} \lambda(x) ( d\mu -\operatorname{Proj}_X(d\pi)) + \int_{Y} \mu(y) (d u - \operatorname{Proj}_Y(d\pi)) \&= \int_{X imes Y} \left ( c(x,y) - \lambda(x) - \mu(y) ight ) d\pi + \int_{X} \lambda(x) d\mu + \int_{Y} \mu(y) d u\end{aligned}L(π,λ,μ)​=∫X×Y​c(x,y)dπ+∫X​λ(x)(dμ−ProjX​(dπ))+∫Y​μ(y)(dν−ProjY​(dπ))=∫X×Y​(c(x,y)−λ(x)−μ(y))dπ+∫X​λ(x)dμ+∫Y​μ(y)dν​

Thus the dual problem can be expressed as:

sup⁡λ,μinf⁡π∈Π(μ,ν)L(π,λ,μ) egin{aligned}\sup_{\lambda, \mu} \inf_{\pi \in \Pi(\mu, u)} L(\pi, \lambda, \mu) \end{aligned}λ,μsup​π∈Π(μ,ν)inf​L(π,λ,μ)​

For any point (x,y)(x,y)(x,y), if c(x,y)−λ(x)−μ(y)<0c(x,y) - \lambda(x) - \mu(y) < 0c(x,y)−λ(x)−μ(y)<0, then the value of L(π,λ,μ)L(\pi, \lambda, \mu)L(π,λ,μ) can be made arbitrarily to −∞-\infty−∞ by increasing π\piπ.

Therefore, the optimal solution must satisfy:

c(x,y)≥λ(x)+μ(y),∀(x,y)∈X×Yc(x,y) \geq \lambda(x) + \mu(y), \quad orall (x,y) \in X imes Yc(x,y)≥λ(x)+μ(y),∀(x,y)∈X×Y

And the dual problem now becomes:

sup⁡λ,μ{∫Xλ(x)dμ+∫Yμ(y)dν:c(x,y)≥λ(x)+μ(y),∀(x,y)∈X×Y} egin{aligned}\sup_{\lambda, \mu} \left \{ \int_{X} \lambda(x) d\mu + \int_{Y} \mu(y) d u : c(x,y) \geq \lambda(x) + \mu(y), orall (x,y) \in X imes Y ight \}\end{aligned}λ,μsup​{∫X​λ(x)dμ+∫Y​μ(y)dν:c(x,y)≥λ(x)+μ(y),∀(x,y)∈X×Y}​

Or more compactly:

sup⁡λ,μ{∫Xλ(x)dμ+∫Yμ(y)dν:λ⊕μ≤c} egin{aligned}\sup_{\lambda, \mu} \left \{ \int_{X} \lambda(x) d\mu + \int_{Y} \mu(y) d u : \lambda \oplus \mu \leq c ight \}\end{aligned}λ,μsup​{∫X​λ(x)dμ+∫Y​μ(y)dν:λ⊕μ≤c}​

The strong duality holds under mild conditions, such XXX and YYY are compact and ccc is continuous. More generally, ccc can be a lower semicontinuous function and exist a feasible π\piπ with finite cost.

Wasserstein Distance

When X=Y,c(x,y)=d(x,y)X = Y, c(x,y) = d(x,y)X=Y,c(x,y)=d(x,y) is a metric, the optimal transport problem induces the Wasserstein distance:

Wp(μ,ν)=inf⁡π∈Π(μ,ν)(∫X×Yd(x,y)p dπ(x,y))1/p egin{aligned}W_p(\mu, u) = \inf_{\pi \in \Pi(\mu, u)} \left( \int_{X imes Y} d(x,y)^p \, d\pi(x,y) ight)^{1/p}\end{aligned}Wp​(μ,ν)=π∈Π(μ,ν)inf​(∫X×Y​d(x,y)pdπ(x,y))1/p​

This distance is a metric on the space of probability measures with finite ppp-th moment, and it satisfies the properties of a metric:

  1. Non-negativity: Wp(μ,ν)≥0W_p(\mu, u) \geq 0Wp​(μ,ν)≥0 for all μ,ν\mu, uμ,ν.
  2. Identity of indiscernibles: Wp(μ,ν)=0W_p(\mu, u) = 0Wp​(μ,ν)=0 if and only if μ=ν\mu = uμ=ν.
  3. Symmetry: Wp(μ,ν)=Wp(ν,μ)W_p(\mu, u) = W_p( u, \mu)Wp​(μ,ν)=Wp​(ν,μ).
  4. Triangle inequality: Wp(μ,ν)+Wp(ν,σ)≥Wp(μ,σ)W_p(\mu, u) + W_p( u, \sigma) \geq W_p(\mu, \sigma)Wp​(μ,ν)+Wp​(ν,σ)≥Wp​(μ,σ) for all μ,ν,σ\mu, u, \sigmaμ,ν,σ.

entry.title: Densest Subgraph
entry.content:
Reference:

  1. A Convex-Programming Approach for Efficient Directed Densest Subgraph Discovery

  2. Efficient and Scalable Directed Densest Subgraph Discovery

UDS problem

Definition

The Undirected Densest Subgraph (DS) problem is defined as follows:

max⁡S⊆VE(S)∣S∣\max_{S\subseteq V} \frac{E(S)}{|S|}S⊆Vmax​∣S∣E(S)​

where E(S)E(S)E(S) is the number of edges in the subgraph induced by SSS.

Linear Program

TBD.

DDS problem

Definition

The Directed Densest Subgraph (DDS) problem is defined as follows:

max⁡S,T⊆VE(S,T)∣S∣∣T∣\max_{S,T\subseteq V} \frac{E(S,T)}{\sqrt{|S||T|}}S,T⊆Vmax​∣S∣∣T∣​E(S,T)​

where E(S,T)E(S,T)E(S,T) is the number of edges from SSS to TTT.

Linear Program

The DDS problem can be formulated as the following linear program:

LP(c):max ∑(u,v)∈Exu,vs.t. ∑v∈Vxuv≤su,∀u∈S∑u∈Vxuv≤tv,∀v∈Txuv≥0,∀(u,v)∈E∑u∈Vsu=ac∑v∈Vtv=bca+b=2\begin{align*}\mathsf{LP}(c):\quad \text{max } & \sum_{(u,v)\in E}x_{u,v} \\\text{s.t. } & \sum_{v\in V} x_{uv} \leq s_u, \forall u\in S \\& \sum_{u\in V} x_{uv} \leq t_v, \forall v\in T \\& x_{uv} \geq 0, \forall (u,v)\in E\\& \sum_{u\in V} s_u = a\sqrt c\\& \sum_{v\in V} t_v = \frac{b}{\sqrt c}\\& a+b = 2\end{align*}LP(c):max s.t. ​(u,v)∈E∑​xu,v​v∈V∑​xuv​≤su​,∀u∈Su∈V∑​xuv​≤tv​,∀v∈Txuv​≥0,∀(u,v)∈Eu∈V∑​su​=ac​v∈V∑​tv​=c​b​a+b=2​

And we have two theorems:

Theorem 1

For a fixed ccc, consider two arbitrary sets PPP,Q⊆VQ\subseteq VQ⊆V, and let c′=∣P∣∣Q∣c'=\frac{|P|}{|Q|}c′=∣Q∣∣P∣​. Then OPT(LP(c))≥2cc′c+c′ρ(P,Q)\text{OPT(LP(c))}\geq \frac{2\sqrt{cc'}}{c+c'}\rho(P,Q)OPT(LP(c))≥c+c′2cc′​​ρ(P,Q).

Theorem 2

Given a feasible solution (x,s,t,a,b)(x,s,t,a,b)(x,s,t,a,b) to LP(c)\text{LP(c)}LP(c), we can construct an (S,T)(S,T)(S,T)-induced subgraph G[S,T]G[S,T]G[S,T] such that abρ(S,T)≥OPT(LP(c))=∑(u,v)∈Exu,v\sqrt{ab}\rho(S,T)\geq \text{OPT(LP(c))}=\sum_{(u,v)\in E}x_{u,v}ab​ρ(S,T)≥OPT(LP(c))=∑(u,v)∈E​xu,v​.

By setting c=∣S∗∣∣T∗∣c=\frac{|S^*|}{|T^*|}c=∣T∗∣∣S∗∣​ in theorem 1, we have:

max⁡cOPT(LP(c))≥ρ(S∗,T∗)\max_c{\text{OPT(LP(c))}}\geq \rho(S^*,T^*)cmax​OPT(LP(c))≥ρ(S∗,T∗)

From theorem 2, we have max⁡cOPT(LP(c))≤abρ(S,T)≤ρ(S,T)≤ρ(S∗,T∗)\max_{c} \text{OPT(LP(c))}\leq\sqrt{ab}\rho(S,T)\leq \rho(S,T)\leq \rho(S^*,T^*)maxc​OPT(LP(c))≤ab​ρ(S,T)≤ρ(S,T)≤ρ(S∗,T∗).

So ρ∗=ρ(S∗,T∗)=max⁡cOPT(LP(c))\rho^*=\rho(S^*,T^*)=\max_{c} \text{OPT(LP(c))}ρ∗=ρ(S∗,T∗)=maxc​OPT(LP(c))

Dual Program

The dual program of the DDS problem can be formulated as follows:

DP(c):min⁡max⁡u∈V{rα(u),rβ(u)}s.t.αu,v+βv,u=1,∀(u,v)∈E2c∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v=rα(u),∀u∈V2c∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u=rβ(v),∀v∈Vαu,v,βv,u≥0∀(u,v)∈E\begin{align*}\mathsf{DP}(c):&&\min && \max_{u \in V} \{r_{\alpha}(u), r_{\beta}(u)\}\\&& \text{s.t.} && \alpha_{u,v}+\beta_{v,u} &=1, && \forall (u,v) \in E\\&& && 2\sqrt{c} \sum_{(u,v) \in E}\alpha_{u,v} &= r_{\alpha}(u), && \forall u \in V \\&& && \frac{2}{\sqrt{c}} \sum_{(u,v) \in E}\beta_{v,u} &= r_{\beta}(v), && \forall v \in V \\&& && \alpha_{u,v}, \beta_{v,u} &\geq 0 && \forall (u,v) \in E\end{align*}DP(c):​​mins.t.​​u∈Vmax​{rα​(u),rβ​(u)}αu,v​+βv,u​2c​(u,v)∈E∑​αu,v​c​2​(u,v)∈E∑​βv,u​αu,v​,βv,u​​=1,=rα​(u),=rβ​(v),≥0​​∀(u,v)∈E∀u∈V∀v∈V∀(u,v)∈E​

Quadratic Program

The dual program can be transformed into a quadratic program (QP) as follows:

QP(c):min⁡c⋅∑u∈Vwα(u)2+1c⋅∑v∈Vwβ(v)2s.t.αu,v+βv,u=1,∀(u,v)∈E∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v=wα(u),∀u∈V∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u=wβ(v),∀v∈Vαu,v,βv,u≥0∀(u,v)∈E\begin{align*}\mathsf{QP}(c):&&\min&&\sqrt c \cdot \sum_{u\in V}w_{\alpha}(u)^2+\frac 1{\sqrt c} \cdot & \sum_{v\in V}w_{\beta}(v)^2 \\&& \text{s.t.}&&\alpha_{u,v}+\beta_{v,u} &=1,&& \forall (u,v) \in E\\&& &&\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\alpha_{u,v}&=w_{\alpha}(u),&& \forall u \in V \\&& &&\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\beta_{v,u}&=w_{\beta}(v),&& \forall v \in V \\&& &&\alpha_{u,v},\beta_{v,u} &\geq 0 && \forall (u,v) \in E\end{align*}QP(c):​​mins.t.​​c​⋅u∈V∑​wα​(u)2+c​1​⋅αu,v​+βv,u​(u,v)∈E∑​αu,v​(u,v)∈E∑​βv,u​αu,v​,βv,u​​v∈V∑​wβ​(v)2=1,=wα​(u),=wβ​(v),≥0​​∀(u,v)∈E∀u∈V∀v∈V∀(u,v)∈E​

entry.title: Random Coordinate Descent Methods
entry.content:
This is a reading note on the paper “Random Coordinate Descent Methods for Minimizing Decomposable Submodular Functions” by A. ENE, Huy L. NGUYEN

arxiv

Knowledge prerequisite on submodular function (in Chinese):

子模函数以及Lovasz拓展

Preliminaries

Let V={1,2,…,n}V = \{1,2,\ldots, n\}V={1,2,…,n}.

Regarding w∈Rnw\in \mathbb{R}^nw∈Rn as a modular function w(A)=∑i∈Awiw(A) = \sum_{i\in A} w_iw(A)=∑i∈A​wi​.

Let F:2V→RF: 2^V \to \mathbb{R}F:2V→R be a submodular function of the form:

F=∑i=1rFiF(∅)=0\begin{align*}F &= \sum_{i=1}^r F_i\\F(\emptyset) &= 0\end{align*}FF(∅)​=i=1∑r​Fi​=0​

where each fif_ifi​ is a simple submodular function.

Additionally, we assume minimizing Fi+wF_i+wFi​+w is easy, i.e., there exists efficient oracles to find the minimizer of Fi+wF_i + wFi​+w for each iii and w∈Rnw\in \mathbb R^nw∈Rn.

We want to minimize FFF.

We pivot to the minimization of the Lovász extension fff.

Let fff be the Lovász extension of FFF written as the support function of the base polytope B(F)B(F)B(F):

f(x)=max⁡w∈B(F)⟨w,x⟩f(x) = \max_{w\in B(F)} \langle w, x \ranglef(x)=w∈B(F)max​⟨w,x⟩

where B(F)={w∈Rn:w(A)≤F(A),∀A⊆V,w(V)=F(V)}B(F) = \{w\in \mathbb{R}^n: w(A) \leq F(A), \forall A\subseteq V, w(V)=F(V)\}B(F)={w∈Rn:w(A)≤F(A),∀A⊆V,w(V)=F(V)}.

And let fif_ifi​ be the Lovász extension of FiF_iFi​, BiB_iBi​ be the base polytope of FiF_iFi​.

The paper considers a proximal version of the problem:

min⁡x∈Rnf(x)+12∥x∥2≡min⁡x∈Rn∑i=1rfi(x)+12r∥x∥2\min_{x\in \mathbb{R}^n} f(x) + \frac{1}{2}\|x\|^2 \equiv \min_{x\in \mathbb{R}^n} \sum_{i=1}^r f_i(x) + \frac{1}{2r}\|x\|^2x∈Rnmin​f(x)+21​∥x∥2≡x∈Rnmin​i=1∑r​fi​(x)+2r1​∥x∥2

Given an optimal solution xxx to the proximal problem, one can obtain an optimal solution to the discrete problem by thresholding xxx at 000; more precisely, the optimal solution to the discrete problem is given by A={i:xi≥0}A = \{i: x_i \geq 0\}A={i:xi​≥0}.

Lemma 1

The dual of the proximal problem is given by:

max⁡y(1)∈B1,…,y(r)∈Br−12∥∑i=1ry(i)∥2.\max_{y^(1)\in B_1, \ldots, y^(r)\in B_r} -\frac{1}{2}\|\sum_{i=1}^r y^{(i)}\|^2.y(1)∈B1​,…,y(r)∈Br​max​−21​∥i=1∑r​y(i)∥2.

The primal and dual variables are related by:

x=−∑i=1ry(i).x = -\sum_{i=1}^r y^{(i)}.x=−i=1∑r​y(i).

Application on Densest Subgraph Problem

To see the definition, please refer to the Densest Subgraph Problem post.

The quadratic formulation of DDS problem is defined as follows:

QP(c):min⁡c⋅∑u∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v)2+1c⋅∑v∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u)2s.t.αu,v+βv,u=1,∀(u,v)∈Eαu,v,βv,u≥0∀(u,v)∈E\begin{align*}\mathsf{QP}(c):&&\min&&\sqrt c \cdot \sum_{u\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\alpha_{u,v}\right)^2+\frac 1{\sqrt c} \cdot & \sum_{v\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\beta_{v,u}\right)^2 \\&& \text{s.t.}&&\alpha_{u,v}+\beta_{v,u} &=1,&& \forall (u,v) \in E\\&& &&\alpha_{u,v},\beta_{v,u} &\geq 0 && \forall (u,v) \in E\end{align*}QP(c):​​mins.t.​​c​⋅u∈V∑​​(u,v)∈E∑​αu,v​​2+c​1​⋅αu,v​+βv,u​αu,v​,βv,u​​v∈V∑​​(u,v)∈E∑​βv,u​​2=1,≥0​​∀(u,v)∈E∀(u,v)∈E​

The dual of such quadratic program is formulated as follows:

Lagrangian relaxation

L=c∑u∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v)2+1c∑v∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u)2+∑(u,v)∈Eλu,v(1−αu,v−βv,u)−∑(u,v)∈Eρu,vαu,v−∑(u,v)∈Eσv,uβv,u\begin{align*}L = & \sqrt{c} \sum_{u\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\alpha_{u,v}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{c}} \sum_{v\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\beta_{v,u}\right)^2 \\& + \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \lambda_{u,v}(1 - \alpha_{u,v} - \beta_{v,u}) \\& - \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \rho_{u,v} \alpha_{u,v} - \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \sigma_{v,u} \beta_{v,u}\end{align*}L=​c​u∈V∑​​(u,v)∈E∑​αu,v​​2+c​1​v∈V∑​​(u,v)∈E∑​βv,u​​2+(u,v)∈E∑​λu,v​(1−αu,v​−βv,u​)−(u,v)∈E∑​ρu,v​αu,v​−(u,v)∈E∑​σv,u​βv,u​​

Now consider the KKT conditions:

KKT Conditions

Stationarity Conditions

∂L∂αu,v=2c∑(u,s)∈Eαu,s−λu,v−ρu,v=0∂L∂βv,u=2c∑(t,v)∈Eβv,t−λu,v−σv,u=0\begin{align*}\frac{\partial L}{\partial \alpha_{u,v}} &= 2\sqrt{c} \sum_{(u,s) \in E}\alpha_{u,s} - \lambda_{u,v} - \rho_{u,v} = 0 \\\frac{\partial L}{\partial \beta_{v,u}} &= \frac{2}{\sqrt{c}} \sum_{(t,v) \in E}\beta_{v,t} - \lambda_{u,v} - \sigma_{v,u} = 0\end{align*}∂αu,v​∂L​∂βv,u​∂L​​=2c​(u,s)∈E∑​αu,s​−λu,v​−ρu,v​=0=c​2​(t,v)∈E∑​βv,t​−λu,v​−σv,u​=0​

Define:

wα(u)=∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v,wβ(v)=∑(u,v)∈Eβv,uw_{\alpha}(u) = \sum_{(u,v) \in E}\alpha_{u,v}, \quad w_{\beta}(v) = \sum_{(u,v) \in E}\beta_{v,u}wα​(u)=(u,v)∈E∑​αu,v​,wβ​(v)=(u,v)∈E∑​βv,u​

Then:

2cwα(u)−λu,v−ρu,v=02cwβ(v)−λu,v−σv,u=0\begin{align*}2\sqrt{c} w_{\alpha}(u) - \lambda_{u,v} - \rho_{u,v} &= 0\\\frac{2}{\sqrt{c}} w_{\beta}(v) - \lambda_{u,v} - \sigma_{v,u} &= 0\end{align*}2c​wα​(u)−λu,v​−ρu,v​c​2​wβ​(v)−λu,v​−σv,u​​=0=0​

⇒{λu,v+ρu,v=2c∑(u,s)∈Eαu,sλu,v+σv,u=2c∑(t,v)∈Eβv,t\begin{align*}\Rightarrow \begin{cases}\lambda_{u,v} + \rho_{u,v} &= 2\sqrt{c} \sum_{(u,s) \in E}\alpha_{u,s}\\\lambda_{u,v} + \sigma_{v,u} &= \frac{2}{\sqrt{c}} \sum_{(t,v) \in E}\beta_{v,t}\end{cases}\end{align*}⇒{λu,v​+ρu,v​λu,v​+σv,u​​=2c​∑(u,s)∈E​αu,s​=c​2​∑(t,v)∈E​βv,t​​​

Primal Feasibility Conditions

αu,v+βv,u=1,∀(u,v)∈Eαu,v,βv,u≥0,∀(u,v)∈E\begin{align*}\alpha_{u,v} + \beta_{v,u} &= 1, && \forall (u,v) \in E \\\alpha_{u,v}, \beta_{v,u} &\geq 0, && \forall (u,v) \in E\end{align*}αu,v​+βv,u​αu,v​,βv,u​​=1,≥0,​​∀(u,v)∈E∀(u,v)∈E​

Dual Feasibility Conditions

ρu,v≥0,∀(u,v)∈Eσv,u≥0,∀(u,v)∈E\begin{align*}\rho_{u,v} &\geq 0, && \forall (u,v) \in E \\\sigma_{v,u} &\geq 0, && \forall (u,v) \in E\end{align*}ρu,v​σv,u​​≥0,≥0,​​∀(u,v)∈E∀(u,v)∈E​

Complementary Slackness Conditions

ρu,v⋅αu,v=0,∀(u,v)∈Eσv,u⋅βv,u=0,∀(u,v)∈E\begin{align*}\rho_{u,v} \cdot \alpha_{u,v} &= 0, && \forall (u,v) \in E \\\sigma_{v,u} \cdot \beta_{v,u} &= 0, && \forall (u,v) \in E\end{align*}ρu,v​⋅αu,v​σv,u​⋅βv,u​​=0,=0,​​∀(u,v)∈E∀(u,v)∈E​

Analysis & Simplification

From the stationarity conditions, we notice that for any vertex uuu, the quantity 2c∑(u,s)∈Eαu,s2\sqrt{c} \sum_{(u,s) \in E}\alpha_{u,s}2c​∑(u,s)∈E​αu,s​ must be equal to λu,v+ρu,v\lambda_{u,v} + \rho_{u,v}λu,v​+ρu,v​ for all edges (u,v)(u,v)(u,v) incident to uuu. This gives us:

λu,v1+ρu,v1=λu,v2+ρu,v2=⋯\lambda_{u,v_1} + \rho_{u,v_1} = \lambda_{u,v_2} + \rho_{u,v_2} = \cdotsλu,v1​​+ρu,v1​​=λu,v2​​+ρu,v2​​=⋯

for all edges (u,v1),(u,v2),…(u,v_1), (u,v_2), \ldots(u,v1​),(u,v2​),… incident to vertex uuu.

Similarly, for any vertex vvv:

λu1,v+σv,u1=λu2,v+σv,u2=⋯\lambda_{u_1,v} + \sigma_{v,u_1} = \lambda_{u_2,v} + \sigma_{v,u_2} = \cdotsλu1​,v​+σv,u1​​=λu2​,v​+σv,u2​​=⋯

Let us define:

xu:=2c∑(u,s)∈Eαu,s,yv:=2c∑(t,v)∈Eβv,tx_u := 2\sqrt{c} \sum_{(u,s) \in E}\alpha_{u,s}, \quad y_v := \frac{2}{\sqrt{c}} \sum_{(t,v) \in E}\beta_{v,t}xu​:=2c​(u,s)∈E∑​αu,s​,yv​:=c​2​(t,v)∈E∑​βv,t​

Then we have:

λu,v+ρu,v=xu,λu,v+σv,u=yv\lambda_{u,v} + \rho_{u,v} = x_u, \quad \lambda_{u,v} + \sigma_{v,u} = y_vλu,v​+ρu,v​=xu​,λu,v​+σv,u​=yv​

Substituting stationarity conditions back to Lagrangian:

inf⁡α,β,wL(α,β,w,λ,ρ,σ)=c∑u∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v)2+1c∑v∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u)2+∑(u,v)∈Eλu,v(1−αu,v−βv,u)−∑(u,v)∈Eρu,vαu,v−∑(u,v)∈Eσv,uβv,u=c∑u∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v)2+1c∑v∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u)2+∑(u,v)∈Eλu,v−∑(u,v)∈E(λu,v+ρu,v)αu,v−∑(u,v)∈E(λu,v+σv,u)βv,u=c∑u∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v)2+1c∑v∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u)2+∑(u,v)∈Eλu,v−∑(u,v)∈E(2c∑(u,s)∈Eαu,s)αu,v−∑(u,v)∈E(2c∑(t,v)∈Eβv,t)βv,u=c∑u∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v)2+1c∑v∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u)2+∑(u,v)∈Eλu,v−2c∑u∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v)2−2c∑v∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u)2=−c∑u∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eαu,v)2−1c∑v∈V(∑(u,v)∈Eβv,u)2+∑(u,v)∈Eλu,v=−14c∑u∈Vxu2−c4∑v∈Vyv2+∑(u,v)∈Eλu,v\begin{align*}\inf_{\alpha,\beta,w}L(\alpha,\beta,w,\lambda,\rho,\sigma) = & \sqrt{c} \sum_{u\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\alpha_{u,v}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{c}} \sum_{v\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\beta_{v,u}\right)^2 \\& + \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \lambda_{u,v}(1 - \alpha_{u,v} - \beta_{v,u}) \\& - \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \rho_{u,v} \alpha_{u,v} - \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \sigma_{v,u} \beta_{v,u}\\= & \sqrt{c} \sum_{u\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\alpha_{u,v}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{c}} \sum_{v\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\beta_{v,u}\right)^2 \\& + \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \lambda_{u,v} \\& - \sum_{(u,v) \in E} (\lambda_{u,v} + \rho_{u,v}) \alpha_{u,v} - \sum_{(u,v) \in E} (\lambda_{u,v} + \sigma_{v,u}) \beta_{v,u}\\= & \sqrt{c} \sum_{u\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\alpha_{u,v}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{\sqrt{c}} \sum_{v\in V}\left(\sum_{(u,v) \in E}\beta_{v,u}\right)^2 \\& + \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \lambda_{u,v} -
